text: OK, two days post-test and the dust is finally settling enough to write something coherent. Belt ceremony was Saturday at Atlas Box CDMX, the gym in Colonia Juárez, and against everything I told myself I would be cool about — I was not cool about it. Profe Esteban tied the black belt himself. He's been my coach for eleven and a half years, and I have wanted this thing for so long that the actual moment of receiving it was disorienting, like the script in my head and the experience in the room weren't lining up. Big news first: my whole family flew in for it. Like, my whole family. Mom flew in from Guadalajara on Friday afternoon, and I picked her up at AICM in the rain — she got off the plane in her good shoes carrying a small duffel and a box of conchas from the panadería near her clinic, because she cannot show up empty-handed even when I told her three times the gym would feed everyone afterward. Dad flew in Saturday morning at 7am with an extra suitcase that I correctly suspected was full of physics books for me to take back to the apartment, even though I have not asked him for any physics books in approximately a decade. Mateo and Sofía drove down from Tijuana, which is twenty-eight hours of highway with a 26-week-pregnant Sofía in the front seat — Mateo says she insisted, says she was not going to miss it for anything, says the doctor cleared her for road travel as long as they stopped every two hours, which is the only reason I let it happen without arguing. They got in Friday night and slept at our apartment in Roma Norte, and Sofía and Camila spent two hours catching up at the kitchen table while Nube did her best blue-heeler impression of being personally responsible for both of them. The ceremony itself: forty-three people in the gym, mats cleared, all the colored belts arranged behind Profe in order. I was at the front. My mom sat in the chair Profe brought out specifically for her — she had told him at some point that she has a back thing — and Camila and dad and Mateo and Sofía and Pablo and Pablo's wife Renata stood behind her. Profe said three sentences about the test the previous Saturday, then said one sentence about the eleven years, then said one more sentence about the kind of student that doesn't quit when the season is hard. He tied the belt. The whole thing took maybe four minutes. The clapping was loud enough that I heard it from inside my own head, like through a thick wall, because my ears were doing the closing-up thing they do when something is too much. Then Profe did the part I had not expected. He walked over to my mom's chair, leaned down so his face was at her level, and said in his low voice in Spanish — which my mom doesn't fully share with him, since he's from Veracruz and she's tapatía, but they made it work — that he wanted to thank her personally for the eleven years of letting me drive an hour each way three times a week to the gym, even when I was a teenager and probably had homework I should have been doing. He said the kid he met at age twenty-one had been raised right. My mom started crying immediately. My dad did the dad thing where he put his hand on her shoulder and just kept it there, like a stabilizer. I did not look at any of this directly because if I had I would have lost it. Afterwards we went to the place on Álvaro Obregón that does the pasta, the one Camila and I take everyone to. Mom sat next to Sofía and they spent half the dinner talking about names and pediatric stuff that I half-listened to. Mateo and dad got into a long argument about something in physics that I did not follow. Pablo gave a small speech about how he met me on day one of jiu-jitsu eleven years ago and never thought I'd outlast him at it. Renata cried. Camila held my hand under the table the whole way through. I had two glasses of wine and stopped because I wanted to actually remember the night, and then I had a third because Mateo poured it for me and I couldn't say no. We got home at almost midnight. Nube met everyone at the door for the second time that day. My mom slept on the pull-out couch. My dad slept on an air mattress in the office because he refuses to take a real bed when offered. Mateo and Sofía got the guest room. Camila and I got our actual bed. The apartment had seven people in it for one night and it felt like a small wedding. The next morning my mom made huevos divorciados for everyone in our small kitchen, ordering me around like I was twelve, and dad and Mateo took Nube out for the long walk to Parque México while Camila and Sofía and I prepped coffee. They flew out separately on Sunday afternoon — mom and dad together back to Guadalajara, Mateo and Sofía driving back to Tijuana with strict instructions to call when they got past Hermosillo. The apartment got very quiet again. Camila and I sat on the couch and didn't say much for a while. The belt is still in the gym bag, which is in the corner of the bedroom, where it goes. I keep almost going to look at it and then deciding I don't need to right now. It's there. It's there. Pablo texted asking if I want to climb at La Cueva on Saturday morning. I said yes. I always say yes to climbing because I always need it more than I think I do. Nube is curled up on the rug behind me and every fifteen minutes she does the dramatic-sigh thing that blue heelers do when they think you should be paying attention to them. I made yerba mate at midnight and Camila made fun of me for it, said it would keep me up. She was right. It always keeps me up. I drink it anyway. Profe Esteban texted me a video of one of the new white belts trying to do a half guard. Took me back. Took me back so far I had to put the phone down for a second. Camila played a Spinetta record on the new turntable while we cooked, and for a moment the apartment felt like it was set in 1976 Buenos Aires. Dad has been re-reading Feynman over the holidays, and he sent me a long voice note about the spinning plate observation as if I hadn't heard it from him five times before. I listened anyway. We watched twenty minutes of a documentary about Argentine asados and Camila got homesick in real time, then we ordered takeout from the place on Álvaro Obregón to compensate. Mom called from Guadalajara, she's still working at the pediatric clinic three days a week, and she asked if I'm sleeping. I lied. I always lie about that with her. Camila got home around eight, kicked her shoes off at the door, and asked what I'd eaten — I had to admit it was just a banana and coffee, and she laughed at me but in the way that means she'll cook. Grandma Lupita's birthday would have been Monday — five years gone now — and I made her champurrado recipe from memory because mom is too sad to do it lately. Profe stopped me after class to ask how the family visit went, and I told him truthfully — best three days I've had in a year. Pablo and I went to the rooftop tacos place on Saturday and ate too much al pastor, then walked it off doing laps around the Parque México. Camila's been deep in a project at Foster — a museum extension in Querétaro — and she came home tonight buzzing about a structural detail she finally cracked. Took Nube out at 6am for the long walk to the park and back, and the air in Roma Norte was that specific cold-blue that only happens in dry-season mornings. Mateo called from Tijuana — Sofía's pregnancy is going well, twenty-six weeks, they're going to find out the gender next week and I told him whatever it is I'm already buying tiny boxing gloves. Mama Lupita would have been ninety-three. I think about what she'd say about the chess board. She'd probably tell me I'm overthinking it. Which I am. Camila played a Spinetta record on the new turntable while we cooked, and for a moment the apartment felt like it was set in 1976 Buenos Aires. I want the finished board to feel heavy in Hugo's hands, weighty enough that he knows it took time, that someone made the decision to make it for him specifically. I'm going to laminate the body in two stages with hide glue because the open time is forgiving and the joint is reversible if I screw up. Profe says you can tell a serious student by what they do on the days nobody is watching. I've been holding onto that one since the test. The Grafana board has fifteen panels now and I keep meaning to clean it up, but every time I try I find a reason to keep one more. I don't know if I'm going to apply to MIT — I haven't told anyone about it yet, not even Camila — but the fact that I'm even considering it means something has shifted. Mama Lupita would have been ninety-three. I think about what she'd say about the chess board. She'd probably tell me I'm overthinking it. Which I am. Hugo Ferreira's birthday is closer than it feels, and the chess board has roughly twelve discrete tasks left, and I am behind. We watched twenty minutes of a documentary about Argentine asados and Camila got homesick in real time, then we ordered takeout from the place on Álvaro Obregón to compensate. I keep wanting to refactor the config loader but I keep telling myself: not while the rollout is in flight. After. After. Code review on Andrés's PR took longer than it should have — I left twelve comments and only three were actually important, the rest were preference. I went back and deleted nine of them. The maker space tools are nicer than what I have at home but the commute eats forty minutes round-trip, so I do prep work at home and run heavy ops there. The deployment pipeline still uses the old GitLab runner, which I want to migrate to GitHub Actions but not this quarter. Adding it to the technical-debt list. I think I'm finally allowing myself to want the harder thing, which is something my younger self never had the bandwidth to admit out loud. The slow accumulation of competence is the only kind that lasts. The rest is fashion. Roberto wants a one-pager for leadership on the rollout, which I'll draft tomorrow. Bullets, not paragraphs. He doesn't read paragraphs.

Body finally getting the message. Two days post-belt and post-family-visit, and the deep tiredness is showing up — the kind that lands in the legs and lower back like a weight you didn't know was suspended. Slept 8.5 hours last night. No 3am wake. Woke up to Nube standing on my chest at 7:40 because Camila had already left for the office and the dog had decided I was on duty. Did some easy mobility work and a short walk. Did not look at any work. Want to keep this floating-feeling for a couple days before the rollout window opens up again. I think I'm finally allowing myself to want the harder thing, which is something my younger self never had the bandwidth to admit out loud.

Back at work today and the rust rewrite is the thing eating most of the headspace. We had the planning meeting this morning at 10am. Andrés led the staging readiness checklist, Carla covered the chaos test plan, I covered the metrics and rollout gates. Roberto sat in the back and asked one question, which is roughly his pattern. The question was about the rollback time. He wanted a number in seconds. I said ninety. He nodded. He left at 10:25. Right now the rust route engine has been building in a feature branch for almost three months. The shape of the migration is: shadow rollout at 1% for a week, then 5%, then 25%, then 50% with full chaos testing turned on, then 100%. We did the shadow rollout last sprint and the numbers were healthy. Today we are five days from the staging cutover, which is the first time real internal traffic — driver dispatch sandbox, ops dashboard pre-prod — touches the rust path in a non-shadow context. I spent most of the afternoon on the metrics infrastructure. The way I'm setting it up: every dispatch decision in the rust path emits a trace span with attributes for input distance, vehicle class, time bucket, and the chosen route's expected duration vs the duration the old Python service would have predicted. Carla wrote a clever Loki query that surfaces the cases where the two predictions diverge by more than ten percent, which is going to be our anomaly hunting tool. We batched up about 400 such cases from the shadow rollout and walked through them in a Wednesday session — 380 of them turned out to be cases where rust was right and Python was sloppy, which is the outcome I had hoped for but had not let myself bet on. The remaining 20 cases were genuinely interesting. Two of them were edge cases involving the pre-1996 vehicle data that has slightly different road-network metadata in our DB, and the rust code was making an assumption that worked for everything in 1996+ but produced strange routes for the older fleet. We patched it. The other 18 were spread between rounding differences (which we accepted as wins for rust because rust was using a more accurate distance calculation), one weird race condition in the trace emitter that does not affect the actual route output, and a handful of cases where the Python side was relying on cached state from a previous request, which the rust path doesn't have. We patched the cache-leak case in the rust path so that we don't introduce a regression in a workload that depends on it. Andrés has been quietly excellent through all of this. He's at the eight-month mark on the team now and is fully owning the metrics pipeline plus the chaos test scaffolding. I told him in his last 1:1 that he's writing better code than I was at his career stage, and he made the face he makes when I compliment him, which is to say he made no face at all and just nodded. He is the most poker-faced engineer I've ever worked with. He also got pulled into a meeting on Monday that he didn't need to be in, and he sent me a Slack DM afterward asking how to politely decline future invitations of that flavor — I told him 'just say no' and he typed back 'lol' and I'm reasonably sure that was his first ever lol. Carla on the other hand is fine but in a different way. She had her baby Camilo six months ago and is still adjusting to the new sleep budget. We're flexible on her hours — she does 7am to 1pm in the office and then takes calls or async work in the late afternoon depending on what Camilo is doing. The chaos test framework she's building is genuinely solid. We agreed today that the staging cutover should not happen on a Monday or Friday — Monday because everyone is groggy from the weekend and Friday because nobody wants to debug something at 4pm when the bar is calling. We picked Wednesday next week. Roberto signed off on the timeline but added that he wants a written go/no-go memo from me by Tuesday at noon. Which is fine. He's a political manager, not a technical one, but he's predictable and I've learned how to write the memo he needs. Three sentences for the recommendation, three bullets for the risks, three bullets for the mitigations, one paragraph at the bottom on what success looks like and the success criteria for backing out. He reads it in three minutes and gives me one of two answers, and I need to make sure both answers are acceptable to me. The rust path has never once shown a memory leak in any of the soak tests we've run, and that fact alone has done more for my confidence than anything else. The Python service had a slow leak that nobody had ever fully diagnosed and that we'd worked around with monthly restarts. The rust path's RSS plateaus within forty minutes of startup and stays there. We had it running in the soak harness for ten days last month and the memory profile was a flat line. Beautiful. I don't know what I expected from rust but apparently the rumors are true. Alright. Going to go take Nube on her walk. The rollout starts in five days and I want to be sleeping eight hours a night when it does. Spent forty minutes on Loki trying to track down a single span that looked weird in the trace. Turned out to be a logging artifact, not a real problem. Code review on Andrés's PR took longer than it should have — I left twelve comments and only three were actually important, the rest were preference. I went back and deleted nine of them. I keep wanting to refactor the config loader but I keep telling myself: not while the rollout is in flight. After. After. Latency histograms look healthier than I expected — the long tail past p99 is now compressed against the body of the distribution, which is the whole point of the rewrite. Andrés has been quietly cleaning up the old Python code paths now that traffic is gone — we'll archive that module next quarter once we're sure nothing else imports from it. The deployment pipeline still uses the old GitLab runner, which I want to migrate to GitHub Actions but not this quarter. Adding it to the technical-debt list. Disk I/O on the read-replica side is the next thing I want to look at — there's a slow query that wakes up every five minutes and we've been ignoring it for too long. I'm logging everything in the runbook in Notion as I go, so the next person who has to debug this isn't doing it from scratch at 2am. The shadow rollout taught us that the old service was actually doing a thing nobody documented — a retry-with-backoff that the new one had to learn to imitate. We had one transient blip at 04:17 that turned out to be a network hiccup on the Mexico City side, not anything we did. PagerDuty fired and resolved itself in 90 seconds. Carla pointed out that we never wrote the chaos test for the new path, so I'm going to do that next week before we forget how the rewrite works. I added a second alert on tail latency specifically because the first one only fires on average and I want to catch the moment a single shard goes weird. The Grafana board has fifteen panels now and I keep meaning to clean it up, but every time I try I find a reason to keep one more. Carla pinged me on Slack to ask if we should add a circuit breaker around the new path; I said yes but probably in the next sprint, not this one. Quiet Tuesday at home. Camila is at the Foster studio late, working on the Querétaro museum extension structural detail she's been chasing. Made arroz con leche from my mom's recipe — she sent the voice note version on Sunday before flying out — and Nube has been doing the polite-but-aggressive sit-stare next to my chair. The apartment in Roma Norte is finally quiet again after the family weekend. Going to read for thirty minutes and then go to sleep. Mateo called from Tijuana — Sofía's pregnancy is going well, twenty-six weeks, they're going to find out the gender next week and I told him whatever it is I'm already buying tiny boxing gloves.

Mid-morning work update. Andrés and I paired for an hour on the metrics emitter, walked through the shadow-rollout dataset and refined two of the queries Carla wrote. We reorganized the dashboard so that the latency panels are in the top row and the error/saturation panels are in the bottom row, which is how I want the on-call engineer to read it during the cutover next week. The change took about forty minutes and required me to re-pin a half-dozen dashboards in our other Grafana instances, but the final version is much easier to scan during an incident. We also caught one bug during the pairing — the trace emitter was double-counting one specific route class because of how the span attributes were being merged. Andrés found it. I would have caught it on the cutover day instead, when the impact would have been a confusing dashboard rather than a quiet bug. Saved us a lot of stress next week. He noticed because the count of one class was visibly higher than the same class's count in production telemetry, and the numbers should match within about five percent in shadow mode. We patched it, redeployed the shadow service, and the count is now back in alignment. Roberto stopped by my desk at 2pm to ask how Tuesday's go/no-go memo was coming. I had drafted it in the morning. Sent him the link to the doc and he said 'looks good, will read tonight.' Which is Roberto for 'I will skim this and probably approve it.' Which is fine. The memo is solid. Three sentences for recommendation. Three bullets for risks: shadow only covers business-hours traffic, rollback procedure has not been chaos-tested, and Andrés is the only one besides me who deeply understands the new path. Three bullets for mitigations: extended shadow window for off-hours traffic this weekend, scheduled chaos-test of the rollback on Thursday evening, and Carla shadowing me during the cutover and writing the postmortem-runbook in real time so we can share knowledge faster. Carla finished the chaos test framework today. We tested it against the staging environment and killed two pods mid-request. The new path handled it like the old path — load balancer notices, sheds traffic, healthy pods absorb the difference, no stranded requests. Beautiful. We are five days from staging cutover and I am cautiously optimistic, which for me is approximately as optimistic as I get during a major rollout. The fact that I've been sleeping is also helping. Chess board status: the body is laminated, the squares are glued up, and I'm into the inlay work on the border. The cedar is behaving better than it did three weeks ago, when I kept getting tear-out on the softer rays — I think I'm finally getting a feel for which way to push the chisel and how much pressure the tool wants. The walnut inlay strips are cut to 1.5mm and they're seating properly into the cedar. I'm using hide glue on the inlay because it's reversible, in case I or Hugo ever needs to repair it twenty years from now. Spent four hours on Sunday at the Roma Norte maker space dialing in the router setup for the inlay channels. The Festool TS 55 REQ has a router attachment that I had not used before, and learning it under deadline pressure is exactly what Profe would have advised against, which is why I gave myself the whole morning to do test cuts on offcuts before touching the actual board. Wound up doing eleven test cuts before I was satisfied. The twelfth one I did on the actual board, holding my breath. It came out clean. The drawer for the magnetic pieces is the part I'm now stuck on. I wanted to do a hand-cut dovetail joint, both because dovetails are pretty and because Hugo will appreciate a hand-cut joint — he taught Camila to make pão de queijo from scratch when she was six because 'machines hide what your hands learned' is something he says — but I am not yet competent enough at hand-cut dovetails to risk it on this board. So the compromise: I'm going to use box joints, which I can cut precisely on the table saw with a jig, and the visual difference is small enough that I think Hugo will not notice unless he is looking for it. Camila said 'he will absolutely notice and he will absolutely love them anyway.' Probably true. Two more weekends of work and I should have the board glue-up done. Then finishing — three coats of beeswax-and-mineral-oil, applied by hand, with sanding between coats. Then the magnetic chess pieces, which I've been 3D-printing in batches at Pablo's friend's lab in Polanco. The pieces are walnut-PLA and have a small neodymium magnet embedded in the base. They click. They click in the way a good piece should click. Spent forty minutes on Loki trying to track down a single span that looked weird in the trace. Climbed at La Cueva this morning with Pablo. He's been chasing a V5 for three weeks and finally sent it. I cheered. I tried it twice, peeled off the second move both times, decided to work on something at my actual level. The gym was packed — the early-bird crew of finance guys plus a youth team coach setting projects for kids. We grabbed pan dulce afterward at the corner panadería and walked back. Good morning. Camila played a Spinetta record on the new turntable while we cooked, and for a moment the apartment felt like it was set in 1976 Buenos Aires. Profe stopped me after class to ask how the family visit went, and I told him truthfully — best three days I've had in a year.

Long Sunday afternoon. Camila is at the studio finishing a model. Nube and I have the apartment, and I've been thinking — really thinking — about what comes next, professionally, after this rust rewrite ships. I have not written this thought down anywhere yet because I have not worked out the shape of it. Putting it here so I can come back to it. I've been at Brújula Logistics for almost four years. The role started as senior backend on a six-engineer team and has become something more like staff engineer for the whole platform, although the title has not officially changed because Roberto's political instincts say titles attract attention and we don't want attention on the salary band right now. I am owning the route engine, the dispatch optimizer, the legacy migration, and most recently the rust rewrite. The work is the most technically interesting work I have ever done and the team is the best team I have ever worked with. I am being paid well. The apartment is comfortable. Camila and I have a life we like. By every external metric, the move would be: stay. And yet. I have been twitching toward something. I cannot fully name it. The closest I can get is: the work is interesting but it is interesting in a vector I have already explored a lot of. Distributed systems. Performance. Latency. Reliability. The rust rewrite has stretched me in some directions but the underlying domain is still 'making a backend run faster and not crash.' I have been doing variations of that for ten years. I am good at it. I will get better at it. But the marginal joy of getting better at it is decreasing for me, not increasing. What has been increasing is my interest in how systems interact with people. Specifically: how do you build software that adapts to the emotional state of the user, that responds to attention and frustration and confidence, that treats the human as a sensor rather than as a generator of clicks? The dispatch system at Brújula has, in a tiny way, hinted at this — drivers' schedules and well-being feed into the optimizer, and we've had good conversations internally about how to do that ethically. But the field is much bigger than what we touch. Affective computing. Attention systems. Wearable bio-signal pipelines. Stuff that lives at the intersection of ML, HCI, and neuroscience. I keep finding myself, on weekends, reading papers from a particular research group at MIT. The group studies how to model affective state from physiological signals — heart rate variability, electrodermal activity, head pose, voice tone — and how to use those models to build interfaces that respond to the human rather than expecting the human to respond to the interface. The group's PI is Rosalind Picard, who founded the field. I have read Affective Computing twice in two years, the second time with notes. I have downloaded fifteen years of papers and read most of them. This is brewing. I haven't told anyone. Not Camila, not my dad, not Pablo, not Profe. Not yet. Because I do not yet know what I want to do about it. The obvious move would be: apply for a PhD. The obstacles to that obvious move are large. I am thirty-three. I have never been in a research environment. I do not have a research advisor. I do not have publications. I would be moving from a senior IC role on a healthy team to a PhD stipend in Cambridge, Massachusetts, which is a 70%+ pay cut and a multi-year time commitment. Camila's job is in CDMX. The dog. The chess board project. The black belt I just earned. And yet the idea will not leave me alone. It came back today during a long shower while Nube sat outside the bathroom door — I caught myself doing the thing my dad does when he's working out a problem in his head, which is rub the back of his neck and stare at the ceiling. I had been staring at the ceiling for ten minutes. Nube was patient. I came out and wrote nothing down because I am not yet ready to write it down anywhere with my real name on it. My dad would understand it the most easily of anyone in my life, but my dad is sixty-six and wants me to stay close to home, in the way that fathers want that. My mom would worry about Camila. Camila would understand it but her career is in Mexico City and the practical question of two careers in two cities for several years is a real one. Pablo would be supportive but his day-to-day is so different from research that the conversation might not land. Profe would say 'do what makes you a more serious person.' I think that is what he would say. OK. I'm going to keep this in the back of my head for now and let the rollout finish at work. After the rollout, when there's some breathing room, I'll write down the actual thinking and start a real conversation with Camila. Not now. Now is for shipping and finishing the chess board and being present at home. The rest can wait six weeks. The slow accumulation of competence is the only kind that lasts. The rest is fashion. Camila's dad Hugo is still grieving Beatriz in his quiet way, and I think the chess board project is partly for him and partly for me, which is the honest version of why I'm doing it. I'm thirty-three and the people I love are getting older and I'm noticing it in small moments — my mom's voice on a phone call, the way my dad takes a beat before answering a complicated question. Five years ago this version of my life would have looked impossibly stable. The amount of work that compounded silently to get here is more than I want to count. I don't know if I'm going to apply to MIT — I haven't told anyone about it yet, not even Camila — but the fact that I'm even considering it means something has shifted. There's a difference between being scared and being unprepared, and the test taught me to feel that difference in my body before my brain catches up. I think I'm finally allowing myself to want the harder thing, which is something my younger self never had the bandwidth to admit out loud. There's a thing my dad used to say — that the work expands to fill the panic budget you give it — and I keep coming back to it on quiet weekends. Profe says you can tell a serious student by what they do on the days nobody is watching. I've been holding onto that one since the test. I keep noticing that the things I find scariest in advance turn out to be the most boring in execution. The rewrite went well because we designed it to go well. The mythical 'launch day adrenaline' was just sitting at a screen looking at numbers go down. The black belt has changed almost nothing about my actual jiu-jitsu, but it has changed a lot about how I walk into rooms in the rest of my life. Mama Lupita would have been ninety-three. I think about what she'd say about the chess board. She'd probably tell me I'm overthinking it. Which I am. When I tell Camila about a problem at work she doesn't try to solve it; she just asks one question that reframes the whole thing. That's a superpower and I should tell her that more often. Thursday night. Camila got home at 8pm with the model still in her head — she'd been working on a structural detail for the Querétaro museum, a tension cable system for the cantilevered overhang, and she walked in and immediately drew it on a napkin to show me. She was tired but in the good tired, the kind where the work has been productive and the body just needs to catch up. We made a quick pasta — orecchiette with chile-toasted breadcrumbs and pancetta — and ate at the kitchen table with Nube doing her best 'i am also here, i should also have orecchiette' routine. Camila's been at Foster + Partners for almost three years now in their CDMX studio. She moved here from São Paulo for the job. Her dad Hugo is in São Paulo still, alone in the apartment, and Camila Skypes with him every Sunday. The Sunday call is sacred. She blocks it on her calendar. Hugo is in the slow-moving phase of grief — Beatriz has been gone for almost three years, but he still has trouble with afternoons, that's what Camila says. The mornings he's fine. The evenings he reads. The afternoons are the hard time. She told me tonight that Hugo asked about me on the call last Sunday. Specifically he asked how the chess board was coming along. I have not said anything to Hugo about the chess board — Camila told him weeks ago and now he asks about it almost every call. So now I am working on a present for someone who knows it is coming and who is asking about it, which has converted the project from a surprise into something more like a shared expectation. I am not sure if this changes how I should think about the project or not. Camila says it does not. She says Hugo is at the age where asking about a thing is its own pleasure, separate from receiving the thing. I think she is probably right. Dad has been re-reading Feynman over the holidays, and he sent me a long voice note about the spinning plate observation as if I hadn't heard it from him five times before. I listened anyway. Camila got home around eight, kicked her shoes off at the door, and asked what I'd eaten — I had to admit it was just a banana and coffee, and she laughed at me but in the way that means she'll cook. Nube is curled up on the rug behind me and every fifteen minutes she does the dramatic-sigh thing that blue heelers do when they think you should be paying attention to them. Profe Esteban texted me a video of one of the new white belts trying to do a half guard. Took me back. Took me back so far I had to put the phone down for a second. Took Nube out at 6am for the long walk to the park and back, and the air in Roma Norte was that specific cold-blue that only happens in dry-season mornings. Mom called from Guadalajara, she's still working at the pediatric clinic three days a week, and she asked if I'm sleeping. I lied. I always lie about that with her. Mateo called from Tijuana — Sofía's pregnancy is going well, twenty-six weeks, they're going to find out the gender next week and I told him whatever it is I'm already buying tiny boxing gloves. Profe stopped me after class to ask how the family visit went, and I told him truthfully — best three days I've had in a year. Pablo and I went to the rooftop tacos place on Saturday and ate too much al pastor, then walked it off doing laps around the Parque México.

Camila threw me a small party on Saturday night, and I am still slightly hungover and very grateful. She had been planning it for weeks without telling me — I learned about it Saturday afternoon when she said 'don't make plans tonight' and gave me her conspiratorial face. I should have known. I did not know. Anyway. Twelve people in the apartment. Pablo and Renata, Profe Esteban and his wife Adriana (who I had only met twice before), Andrés (who came alone because he is not currently dating and is uncomfortable being asked about it), Carla and her partner Joaquín (Camilo the baby was with Carla's mother for the night), Camila's coworker Mariana, and a sister-of-Mariana visiting from Querétaro who turned out to be a chess player and who spent most of the night talking with Profe about strategy in a way that I'm fairly sure baffled Pablo and delighted Profe. Twelve people plus Nube, who treated the party as her personal social event and did fifteen minutes of meet-and-greet at every door knock. Camila had set the apartment up beautifully. She'd hung small white lights on the kitchen pass-through. She had pulled out the good plates that we never use. She had bought three bottles of mezcal Don Mateo del Valle and one bottle of cachaça (for the Brazilian touch she likes to slip in), and she'd had Mariana help her prep about a dozen small plates — guacamole, cured tuna with lime, slices of plantain with crema, the panela cheese and mango salsa thing she does, plus a vat of pozole that simmered all afternoon and made the apartment smell incredible. The pozole was the killer move. Pozole on a chilly CDMX night with twelve people in your apartment is a kind of love language. Profe arrived first at 7:30pm with Adriana, who I had only briefly met at a gym anniversary years ago and who turned out to be wonderful — a children's book illustrator, bilingual in Spanish and Portuguese (her mother is from Belém), with strong opinions about Mexican mezcal. She and Camila bonded almost instantly. Within thirty minutes they were in the kitchen comparing notes on Brazilian and Mexican Christmas traditions and laughing at something I couldn't quite hear. Pablo and Renata came at 8pm with a bottle of red and a small wrapped gift, which turned out to be a black-and-white framed photo from our first jiu-jitsu tournament eleven years ago — I had completely forgotten this photo existed. We are both in white belts. I am 22 years old in the photo, twenty pounds heavier than I am now, with a haircut that should not have been allowed. Pablo is grinning. He had won that day. I had lost in the second round to a guy from Veracruz who I would later spend three years trying to beat and finally beating in 2019. The photo is now on the bookshelf next to the espresso machine and I keep almost looking at it and almost not believing that the kid in it is me. Andrés arrived at 8:15pm with a small bag of chocolates from a place on Reforma that I had never heard of but apparently is the place for chocolates. He took one look at the room, said 'oh,' and immediately tried to position himself near a wall where he would be slightly less visible. Camila had me steer him toward Profe, who took one look at Andrés and said 'come on, come over here' in the tone Profe uses with new white belts, and within ten minutes Andrés was telling Profe about a chess opening he'd been working through. Andrés plays chess online at around the 1900 ELO mark, which I had not known. They talked for forty minutes. Andrés left the corner. He laughed twice. Carla, when she arrived at 8:30, looked at Andrés in the middle of the room and said 'is that Andrés' and gave me the look that meant 'how did you accomplish this.' Carla brought Joaquín, who is calm in the way that you become calm when you have a six-month-old at home. Joaquín works in marketing for a hotel group and is very different from Carla in personality but somehow the match works. He talked to Pablo about climbing for an hour. Pablo gave him the same speech he gives me roughly every six months, which is that climbing is the last honest sport because the wall does not care who you are or what you do for a living. Joaquín nodded politely. I do not think he is going to start climbing. At 9:45 Camila tapped a glass and said she wanted to say something. I tried to leave the room. She physically grabbed my arm and pulled me back. The speech was short. She said: eleven years. She said: the first time she met me, she met me at a dinner party at Mariana's apartment and I had spent half the night talking about the differences between gi and no-gi training, and she had thought 'this man is exhausting.' She said: she was wrong. She said: the thing she has learned about me in five years is that the depth and consistency are real, and that the belt is not a trophy, it's a marker. She said: the marker is for showing what kind of long-haul person I am. I lost it. I lost it in front of twelve people in my own kitchen. Camila pretended not to notice. Profe pretended not to notice. Pablo, kind man, made a loud joke about something else to give me thirty seconds to recover. Adriana came over to me afterwards and put her hand on my arm and said in Portuguese 'querido, é bom chorar.' I'd later ask Camila to translate. It means 'dear, it is good to cry.' They left between 11 and midnight. Andrés got an Uber home. Profe and Adriana stayed last, helping us clean up despite our protests. Profe insisted on washing the pozole pot while Adriana dried. He did not say much during the washing but at the door, before leaving, he held my shoulder for an extra beat and said 'estás bien, mijo.' Which is Profe for 'you are doing okay.' Which from Profe is a lot. Camila and I sat on the couch at 12:30 with the apartment looking like a small wedding had happened. Nube was asleep under the coffee table, exhausted from being a host. We did not talk much. We just sat there with the white lights still on, watching the candles burn down, smelling the leftover pozole. I held her hand. I'm holding it now, in a manner of speaking, by writing this down. The party was the best thing. She is the best thing. End of entry. Monday standup at 9:30. Roberto sat in for the first time in three weeks. He likes to drop into standups before any major rollout, partly to be visible and partly to flag any political concern he wants on the team's radar. Today he flagged that the COO had asked about the rollout timing because of the quarterly board meeting on Thursday. Translated from Roberto-speak: do not ship anything that might cause a P0 on Thursday morning. We had already planned the staging cutover for Wednesday at 11am, deliberately slotted to give us 36 hours of buffer before the board meeting, so this is fine. Andrés walked through the readiness checklist. Out of 47 items, 44 are green, 2 are yellow, 1 is red. The red is the chaos test of the rollback procedure — we have not yet practiced backing out. Carla and I are pairing on this Wednesday morning before the cutover. The two yellows are: extended off-hours shadow data (Carla has a 96-hour collection window running and the data so far looks good but is not yet complete), and the runbook's section on rollback decision-making (which I'm cleaning up tonight). After standup I spent two hours reviewing Andrés's last PR in the rust path — a refactor of the trace emitter that simplifies the code by about 40 lines and makes the merging logic less likely to bug out the way it did last week. I left three comments. One was a real concern, two were nits. He addressed the real concern within an hour and pushed the merge by lunch. We are in good shape. Lunch with Camila at the place on Álvaro Obregón. She had a sandwich and I had the mushroom soup. We did not talk about work. We talked about a book she's reading on Niemeyer's late period. Came back to the office, wrote the rollback runbook section, left at 6:30pm. Quiet, productive day. Tomorrow more memo work and a final rehearsal of the cutover steps. Wednesday is the big day. The deployment pipeline still uses the old GitLab runner, which I want to migrate to GitHub Actions but not this quarter. Adding it to the technical-debt list. Carla pointed out that we never wrote the chaos test for the new path, so I'm going to do that next week before we forget how the rewrite works. I'm logging everything in the runbook in Notion as I go, so the next person who has to debug this isn't doing it from scratch at 2am. Disk I/O on the read-replica side is the next thing I want to look at — there's a slow query that wakes up every five minutes and we've been ignoring it for too long. Roberto wants a one-pager for leadership on the rollout, which I'll draft tomorrow. Bullets, not paragraphs. He doesn't read paragraphs. The shadow rollout taught us that the old service was actually doing a thing nobody documented — a retry-with-backoff that the new one had to learn to imitate. I added a second alert on tail latency specifically because the first one only fires on average and I want to catch the moment a single shard goes weird. We had one transient blip at 04:17 that turned out to be a network hiccup on the Mexico City side, not anything we did. PagerDuty fired and resolved itself in 90 seconds. Carla pinged me on Slack to ask if we should add a circuit breaker around the new path; I said yes but probably in the next sprint, not this one. Andrés has been quietly cleaning up the old Python code paths now that traffic is gone — we'll archive that module next quarter once we're sure nothing else imports from it.

Roberto signed off on the go/no-go memo this morning at 9:14am. He left two comments. The first asked if I had run the rollback chaos test yet — I told him no, that's tomorrow morning. He said 'do it before the cutover or move the cutover,' which is exactly the answer I expected and exactly what the plan already was. The second comment was about external comms: he wants a Slack post in the engineering channel right after cutover saying 'rust path is now the default' in plain language so non-rust-aware engineers know what to expect if they're debugging. I had not thought of this. Good catch on his part. I'll write it. Spent the rest of the morning rehearsing the cutover steps. We have 23 distinct steps in the runbook, each timed and each with an assigned owner. The steps fall into four phases: pre-flight checks, traffic shift from 50% to 100%, monitoring window, and post-cutover validation. I walked through it twice on paper and then once in the staging environment with the actual tooling. The whole sequence takes about 90 minutes if everything is healthy. We sketched out what 'his' project might look like — most likely the migration of the legacy reporting pipeline to a more modern setup, which is a bounded scope but technically meaty. He said yes. I'll talk to Roberto about it after this rollout. Got home at 6:45pm. Camila was already there. We watched an episode of a Brazilian crime show with subtitles for me, ate leftover pozole, walked Nube to the corner and back. Light night. Wednesday morning. Got into the office at 7am for the chaos test of the rollback procedure. Carla was already there with two coffees, one of which she handed to me without asking. We did the test against staging at 7:15. The procedure: simulate a P0 in the new rust path, run the rollback runbook step-by-step, measure end-to-end time from decision to recovery. The test went fine. Better than fine. End-to-end time from 'declare incident' to 'rollback complete and traffic on Python path' was 71 seconds. The bottleneck was a manual confirmation step that we kept in the procedure for safety — without it the time would have been about 45 seconds. We left the manual step in. Some friction is good when you're rolling back. Carla had built a small Slack bot for the chaos test that posts updates to a private channel with the timing data, which made the test much easier to review afterwards. We ran the test twice to confirm reproducibility. Both runs landed within five seconds of each other. Logged the results. Updated the runbook. Done. At 11am we did the actual cutover. Forty-four people in the engineering Slack channel. Roberto in the war room (also Slack). I did the steps. Andrés watched the metrics. Carla watched the error budget. The traffic shift took six minutes — we ramped from 50% to 100% in three increments, watching for anomalies between each. No anomalies appeared. We hit 100% at 11:14am. The next forty-five minutes I just sat at my screen watching numbers. Latencies dropped, as expected. Error rates flat. Memory profile flat. CPU lower than the Python service was using, by a noticeable margin. We held at 100% with full eyes-on for two hours. Nothing happened. Nothing was supposed to happen. The cutover was clean. Posted to the engineering channel at 1:30pm: 'rust path is now the default. dispatch decisions now go through the rust route engine. legacy python path is still warm and can be recovered in 90 seconds if needed.' Wednesday afternoon, 3pm. Three hours since the cutover at 100%. The dashboards are all green. P50 latency is at 44ms, which is below where Python was sitting at 78ms. P99 is at 189ms vs Python's 412ms. Tail behavior is dramatically better. Memory is flat. CPU is at 23% per pod vs Python's 41%. Error rate is unchanged and very low. This is the best post-cutover hour I have ever experienced. I keep waiting for something to go wrong. I have my muscle memory primed for the rollback. Nothing is happening. Andrés keeps refreshing the dashboard and saying 'still flat.' Carla finally said at 2:45pm 'sebas, it's done. it's working. go for a walk.' I went for a walk. Walked around the building twice. Came back. Still flat. The afternoon stretched into the evening with no incident. Disk I/O on the read-replica side is the next thing I want to look at — there's a slow query that wakes up every five minutes and we've been ignoring it for too long. Latency histograms look healthier than I expected — the long tail past p99 is now compressed against the body of the distribution, which is the whole point of the rewrite. Carla pointed out that we never wrote the chaos test for the new path, so I'm going to do that next week before we forget how the rewrite works. I added a second alert on tail latency specifically because the first one only fires on average and I want to catch the moment a single shard goes weird.

Thursday, post-cutover day one. I went into the office at 9am, looked at the dashboards for ten minutes, and confirmed that nothing had moved overnight except the small overnight traffic spike from the 6am pre-rush hour deliveries, which the rust path absorbed cleanly. Sent a status update to Roberto. He replied 'great.' That is now two compliments from Roberto in two days, which is unprecedented. Spent the morning catching up on PR reviews I had been ignoring during the rollout — six of them in queue from various team members across the org, none of them blocking but all of them deserving of attention. Worked through them. One refactor I had to push back on (it would have introduced a circular dependency between two services that we have been very careful to keep decoupled). The other five I approved with minor comments. At noon I had a PT appointment at Clínica Querétaro — back has been at the steady-state 70% for several weeks now, which is the best it's been since the back tweak last year. Dra. Reyes had me do the new resistance band exercise plus the single-leg bridges and a new one with a foam roller. She said the back has 'consolidated,' which is her way of saying I have hit a plateau and we should re-evaluate the program. We agreed I'll keep doing the home program for two months and then we'll reassess whether to taper or to add new exercises. Came back to the office at 2pm. Andrés had spotted a small issue — not a bug, more of a logging optimization — and we paired on it for an hour to clean it up. Left the office at 5:15. The board meeting at the COO level happened at 4pm and apparently went well, with the rollout being mentioned as an example of successful execution. Roberto told me this on the way out. He looked pleased. Home by 6pm. Quiet evening. Read for an hour. Camila came home at 8pm. We made dinner together, talked about her week. Slept by 11. I keep noticing that the things I find scariest in advance turn out to be the most boring in execution. The rewrite went well because we designed it to go well. The mythical 'launch day adrenaline' was just sitting at a screen looking at numbers go down. I'm thirty-three and the people I love are getting older and I'm noticing it in small moments — my mom's voice on a phone call, the way my dad takes a beat before answering a complicated question. Camila's dad Hugo is still grieving Beatriz in his quiet way, and I think the chess board project is partly for him and partly for me, which is the honest version of why I'm doing it. There's a difference between being scared and being unprepared, and the test taught me to feel that difference in my body before my brain catches up. Profe says you can tell a serious student by what they do on the days nobody is watching. I've been holding onto that one since the test. The black belt has changed almost nothing about my actual jiu-jitsu, but it has changed a lot about how I walk into rooms in the rest of my life. Mama Lupita would have been ninety-three. I think about what she'd say about the chess board. She'd probably tell me I'm overthinking it. Which I am. Five years ago this version of my life would have looked impossibly stable. The amount of work that compounded silently to get here is more than I want to count. When I tell Camila about a problem at work she doesn't try to solve it; she just asks one question that reframes the whole thing. That's a superpower and I should tell her that more often.

Friday afternoon at the maker space, finally back on the chess board after the rust rewrite consumed every spare hour for two weeks. Got there at 1pm and stayed until 6pm, which is the longest single session I've had on this project. Today's task: cut the box joints for the storage drawer. I made a jig for the box joints last weekend and tested it on offcuts of poplar. The jig worked. The box joints came out tight. Today I cut them in walnut, which is what the actual drawer is built from. Walnut behaves differently from poplar — denser, harder, less forgiving on the chip-out. I had to slow the saw blade and feed more carefully. The first joint came out clean. The second joint came out with a small bit of tear-out on the back side, which is fixable but annoying. The third and fourth joints came out clean. I dry-fit the drawer at 4pm. The fit is tight enough that I needed a mallet to get the corners home. Slightly too tight, actually — I'll need to take a couple of thou off one face with a hand plane before I glue up. Better too tight than too loose, in any case. Spent the last hour on the magnetic chess pieces. Pablo's friend at the lab in Polanco printed another batch on Wednesday, and I picked them up Friday morning. The print quality is better on this batch — Pablo's friend tweaked the slicing parameters to reduce the layer lines, and the result is noticeably smoother. I'm sanding each piece by hand on a soft pad before doing the final wax finish. The sanding takes about four minutes per piece. There are 32 pieces. I have three of them done. So that's a lot of evenings at the kitchen table ahead of me. Hugo's birthday is in five weeks. I have the body of the board done, the inlay done, the drawer cut and ready to glue, and 3 of 32 pieces sanded. The finish work — three coats of beeswax and mineral oil with cure time between coats — needs at least ten days of calendar. I am behind. I will catch up on weekends. Spent forty minutes on Loki trying to track down a single span that looked weird in the trace. Turned out to be a logging artifact, not a real problem. Roberto wants a one-pager for leadership on the rollout, which I'll draft tomorrow. Bullets, not paragraphs. He doesn't read paragraphs. Carla pinged me on Slack to ask if we should add a circuit breaker around the new path; I said yes but probably in the next sprint, not this one. The deployment pipeline still uses the old GitLab runner, which I want to migrate to GitHub Actions but not this quarter. Adding it to the technical-debt list. I keep wanting to refactor the config loader but I keep telling myself: not while the rollout is in flight. After. After. The shadow rollout taught us that the old service was actually doing a thing nobody documented — a retry-with-backoff that the new one had to learn to imitate. Disk I/O on the read-replica side is the next thing I want to look at — there's a slow query that wakes up every five minutes and we've been ignoring it for too long. The Grafana board has fifteen panels now and I keep meaning to clean it up, but every time I try I find a reason to keep one more. I'm logging everything in the runbook in Notion as I go, so the next person who has to debug this isn't doing it from scratch at 2am.

Lazy Saturday morning. Camila woke up before me and made pão de queijo from scratch — her mom's recipe — and the apartment smells incredible. Nube is doing the dramatic-stare thing because she knows pão de queijo and is hoping for a corner of one. We're going to walk to the market on Cuauhtémoc later and pick up vegetables for a slow lunch. Quiet day. Only chess board work scheduled. No work. Camila played a Spinetta record on the new turntable while we cooked, and for a moment the apartment felt like it was set in 1976 Buenos Aires. Pablo texted asking if I want to climb at La Cueva on Saturday morning. I said yes. I always say yes to climbing because I always need it more than I think I do.

Sunday call with mom. She caught me up on the family — Tía Marisol in the hospital briefly for blood pressure (she's home now and fine), Mateo and Sofía's baby news (still 26 weeks, healthy), and dad has been on a Feynman re-read kick which I already knew about. Mom asked again about the chess board. She always asks about projects. She told me Mama Lupita would have approved of the project and I had to put the phone down for a second. Hung up at 4pm. Profe Esteban texted me a video of one of the new white belts trying to do a half guard. Took me back. Took me back so far I had to put the phone down for a second.

Sunday afternoon at the maker space. Glued up the drawer. Used Old Brown Glue (hide glue), which has open time of about 15 minutes at room temperature, which is forgiving for a four-corner glue-up if you've got your clamps prepped. I had my clamps prepped. I had eight clamps. I wound up using six. Glue-up went smoothly. The corners came together square. I checked diagonals with a tape measure in both directions and got 0.5mm difference, which is well within tolerance for a hand-built drawer. I left it in clamps for two hours, which is overkill for hide glue but the maker space wasn't busy and I had nowhere else to be. While the glue cured I sanded another six chess pieces. Six down, twenty-six to go. I'm getting faster — three minutes per piece now instead of four — but it's still a lot of evening hours. I bought an audiobook on the history of the Mexican railway specifically to listen to during sanding. Weirdly compelling. After the drawer came out of the clamps I dry-fit it into the storage compartment of the chess board body. It seats well — slides in and out smoothly, no binding, no slop. The drawer has a small recess at the front that I'll cut a finger pull into next weekend. I had thought about adding a brass ring pull but decided against it; Hugo's aesthetic is restrained and a brass ring would feel decorative in the wrong way. The finger recess is more in keeping with what he likes. Camila's been at the studio late this weekend. She's racing a deadline on the Querétaro museum extension. I made dinner solo on Saturday night — black bean chili and rice, which is my Sunday-prep dish — and saved her a portion for tonight. She'll be home by nine. We'll eat together. Need to write this down because I've been avoiding writing it down. The insomnia is back. Worse than the test prep version. I'm down to four to five hours a night, on a more or less regular pattern, and it's been like this for nearly two weeks now — it started about three days after the cutover, when the work pressure should have been releasing. Instead the wake time crept earlier and the back-to-sleep got harder. Pattern: I fall asleep fine. Usually around 11pm. I sleep until somewhere between 2:45 and 3:30am. I wake up. I am instantly fully awake — not groggy, not half-conscious. Wired. Heart rate elevated. Mind doing the racing thing. I lie still for thirty minutes, sometimes an hour, and either I drift back off into a light unrestful sleep until 6:30 or I don't, and on the don't-nights I get up at 4am and read in the kitchen with the lamp on so I don't wake Camila. This is different from the test-prep insomnia in two ways. First, the test-prep insomnia made sense — there was a thing I was anxious about, the test was on the calendar, the worry was tracked to a real event. This one has no such target. The cutover went well. The post-cutover monitoring went well. By every external metric the work pressure is at its lowest in months. And yet the body has decided, without my permission, that it's anxious about something. Second difference: the test-prep version, when I lay there at 3am, my mind would replay test scenarios. Specific drills. Specific opponents. Specific weaknesses. This one — and this is the part that has been bothering me — my mind goes to the future. Not the immediate future. The medium-term future. What am I doing in five years? Should I still be at Brújula? Why have I been reading those affective computing papers every weekend? Why does the idea of a research environment make my chest tight in the way wanting something makes a chest tight, and not in the way fearing something makes a chest tight? Those are the thoughts. Round and round. Camila has noticed. She would have to be unobservant to not notice. She has been unbelievably gentle about it. She has not pressed me. She has only said 'you'll tell me when you're ready' in the kind of way that makes me want to tell her now. I have not yet because I don't yet have a coherent thing to tell. I have a vague pull and a worsening sleep schedule and a pattern of weekend paper-reading that I have not previously discussed with anyone. That is not a coherent thing. That's a symptom. The back-pain history is making this harder to ignore than I'd like. I learned during PT last year that for me, when something is wrong emotionally, the back goes first. The sleep going bad is a second-order signal that something has been wrong for a while and I haven't addressed it. The first-order signal would have been the emotional thing itself, which I should have seen earlier. I have an option here. I can talk to Camila this weekend. I can tell her what I have been quietly reading. I can lay out the scary obvious move — applying for a PhD — and let her tell me what she actually thinks. Not what she thinks I want to hear. What she thinks. Camila is the most direct person I know about her own preferences once she knows you want the unfiltered version, and I have spent five years learning to ask for the unfiltered version. I am going to do that this weekend. Putting it on paper here so I commit to it. Saturday night, after dinner, when we're on the couch, I will say: hey, can I tell you what's been keeping me up? She will say of course. I will tell her. We will see what happens. I am not asking permission. I am asking what she sees, what she thinks, what concerns her, what excites her. Then we'll figure out what we do with whatever the conversation produces. In the meantime: tonight I am going to take a melatonin (1mg, my old test-prep dose), drink chamomile, get into bed by 10:15pm, and if I wake at 3am I will get up immediately rather than fight it. Reading in the kitchen at 3:30am has been less destructive to my sleep architecture than lying in bed forcing it. Pablo would say 'go to the gym instead and burn it off' but the back will not appreciate jiu-jitsu at 4am, and I am old enough now to know that. Notes for whoever reads this later: the body knows things before the mind does. The body has been saying something for two weeks. The mind has been refusing to translate. The translation is happening this weekend whether I want it or not. There's a thing my dad used to say — that the work expands to fill the panic budget you give it — and I keep coming back to it on quiet weekends. The slow accumulation of competence is the only kind that lasts. The rest is fashion. Five years ago this version of my life would have looked impossibly stable. Big update. RouteOpt — the broader name we use internally for the rust route engine plus the optimizer pipeline — officially shipped to staging at month 16 of the project. Today is the day. We threw a small pizza thing at 5pm in the office. About twenty engineers showed up plus Roberto plus the COO came down for fifteen minutes which is the kind of thing he does only when something has gone right. I gave a five-minute talk that I had not prepared, which is the right way to do five-minute talks. The headline metric: the p95 latency on dispatch decisions has dropped from 340 milliseconds on the old Python service to 38 milliseconds on the rust path. From 340 to 38. That is roughly a 9x improvement on p95. Memory consumption has dropped 60%. CPU is down 44%. Error rate is unchanged at our already-low baseline, which means we have not introduced any new failure modes in exchange for the speed. This is the metric Roberto wanted to lead with in the leadership readout. Three figures. Two of them are reductions, one of them is unchanged. Roberto loves this kind of slide. He likes 'we made this faster, smaller, and didn't break it.' He doesn't like ambiguity. We gave him no ambiguity. How we got here: it's been 16 months from the first 'should we rewrite this in rust' conversation to the staging cutover. The first six months were mostly skepticism — Andrés and I built a tiny prototype and convinced ourselves it was worth trying, then convinced Carla, then convinced Roberto, then convinced his boss. The middle six months were design and implementation, which involved a lot of rewriting, a lot of throwing away of approaches that turned out to be bad, and one major architectural pivot at month 8 when we realized we'd been wrongly modeling the route problem as graph traversal when it was actually a constrained optimization problem. The pivot cost us six weeks of calendar but saved us about three months downstream. The last four months have been hardening, shadow rollout, chaos testing, and the cutover. Looking back at the design doc I wrote in month 1 — and I just dug it up — it's interesting to see what I got right and what I got wrong. I correctly predicted that the rust path would be 5-10x faster on p95. I incorrectly predicted that memory would be 'about the same.' Memory turned out to be much better than that, partly because rust gives you tighter control over allocation patterns and partly because we redesigned the data structures to be more compact during the rewrite. I correctly predicted that we'd hit a major architectural realization midway through (I called it the 'month 6 surprise' and it turned out to be the month 8 surprise, which is close enough). I incorrectly predicted the rollout would take six weeks; it took twelve. The team is in great shape. Andrés is confident and clearly ready for his own project next quarter — the legacy reporting pipeline migration is the candidate. Carla is back at full capacity after her parental leave and has been writing some of the best chaos test code I've ever read; she's been talking about wanting to move toward an SRE-leaning role and I am thinking through how to structure that. Roberto has been politically supportive in exactly the way I needed him to be, which is mostly by getting out of the way and translating leadership concerns into bullet points I could address. What's next at work, project-wise: we have to harden the staging cutover into a full production cutover, which means about 4-6 more weeks of monitoring and adjustment. After that the rust path replaces the Python path entirely and we can begin removing the Python service from the architecture, which is a project of its own (the Python service has tendrils into about seven other internal services and unwinding those tendrils will take care). I do not yet know whether I want to lead that unwinding or hand it to Andrés. We will see. The COO came down to the pizza party at 5:15pm and stayed for about fifteen minutes. He is not someone I have spent much time with — he's two layers up from me — and he was warmer than I expected. He said 'thank you' twice, which from him is roughly equivalent to a parade. He asked Andrés a question about the architecture and Andrés answered it well, which made me happy. He left and we ate pizza for another forty minutes and Carla showed me a picture of Camilo in a tiny costume from a recent photoshoot. Came home at 7:30pm. Camila could see I was lit up. We made a small celebratory dinner — she opened a bottle of red — and I told her every detail of the day. She listened the way she always listens. She asked at one point what was next for me, in the bigger sense. I told her: I want to talk about it on Saturday. She said okay. She did not press. We watched something light, went to bed at eleven. Wednesday evening at the maker space. Cut the finger pull into the front of the drawer. OK. Saturday night happened. Writing this on Sunday morning while Camila is still asleep and Nube is at my feet. We had dinner at home — Camila had bought ribeye at the place on Insurgentes, she made a chimichurri, I did a cauliflower thing, we ate at the kitchen table with one candle and the lamp dimmed. We talked about light things for the first hour. Mariana is dating someone new. Pablo is thinking about moving to a bigger apartment. Sofía's pregnancy is going well, 28 weeks, they're going to do a small thing in Tijuana when she's at 32 weeks for safety. Hugo had his doctor's appointment, all is fine. Around 9:15pm we moved to the couch with herbal tea. I had said earlier in the week that I wanted to talk about something this weekend. Camila knew that was tonight. She put her phone face down on the coffee table, which is her tell that she's giving me her undivided. I told her. I told her about the papers. About Picard. About the affective computing field. About the fifteen years of papers I have downloaded and read. I told her I had been thinking about applying for a PhD. I told her I had been thinking about MIT specifically. I told her I had not told anyone yet — not Pablo, not my dad, not Profe — and that the not-telling had been part of why I haven't been sleeping. I told her I did not have a plan, just a pull, and that I wanted to know what she actually thought. Not what she thought I wanted to hear. The real thing. She listened the whole way through without interrupting. When I was done she sat with it for maybe a minute, which from her is a lot of pause. Then she said three things. First, she said: 'I have known you've been brewing on this for about a year.' This stunned me. I asked how. She said the way I read on weekends had changed. The way I talked about work had changed. The way I held my phone and scrolled in the evenings had changed. She had been waiting. She is observant in a way that I have never been observant. Second, she said: 'I am not surprised, and I am not against it.' She said the not-against-it carefully. She said her concerns are practical. She said her job is in CDMX and her firm has no Boston presence, and that the architecture community in Boston is small and competitive. She said the dog. She said her dad. She said the chess board project, which I have been working on for him, and which she did not want to interrupt. She said money. She said time — that a PhD is at least five years and probably more. Third, she said something that I had not been ready for. She said: 'I want you to be the version of yourself that is most alive. I have watched you be a version of yourself that is alive but not at maximum. I want maximum.' Her exact words. Maximum. She said: 'I think this is your maximum.' She said: 'we'll figure out the practical things. but you have to apply. you have to know.' I cried. I did not expect to cry. I had thought the conversation would be a negotiation. It was not a negotiation. It was a green light delivered with the grace of someone who had already done the thinking and had been waiting for me to do mine. We talked through practical questions for another two hours. The list: when would the application cycle be, who would the recommenders be, what would my research statement say, what would Camila do during the application year, what would Camila do during the PhD itself, would she stay in CDMX and visit, would she try to find a way to move part of the time, what about the dog, what about the apartment, what about the family. We did not solve all of these. We made a list of them. The list is on the kitchen counter as I write this. At one point she said: 'when I was 28 I left São Paulo for CDMX with no plan and an architecture degree and one apartment lead. It worked. The thing about big moves is that the work expands to absorb them.' She said her dad would understand. She said she would tell her dad after the chess board is delivered, not before, because it would unsettle him to know two big things at once. We went to bed at midnight. I slept for the first time in two weeks at six and a half hours. Woke at 6:30 to Nube's standard 'hello it is morning' face. Camila is still asleep next to me as I write this. She does not know yet that I slept. She will be pleased. What I'm taking from the conversation: I have been carrying this alone for a year without realizing it was eating me. The body knew. Camila knew, in her way. I had been the slowest person in the room to figure out what was happening. Plan: I'm going to start writing the research statement this week. Slowly. No deadline yet. I'll target a December application for fall start the year after, which gives me eight months to get the application together and to figure out the practical stuff with Camila. Eight months is a long time. We can plan a lot in eight months. Going to make Camila coffee now. Grandma Lupita's birthday would have been Monday — five years gone now — and I made her champurrado recipe from memory because mom is too sad to do it lately. Camila played a Spinetta record on the new turntable while we cooked, and for a moment the apartment felt like it was set in 1976 Buenos Aires. Profe Esteban texted me a video of one of the new white belts trying to do a half guard. Took me back. Took me back so far I had to put the phone down for a second. Discovered something unsettling on the chess board this morning. Writing it down now while the panic is fresh. I had finished the sanding on the board body Friday night and left it on the bench in the spare room overnight, with the intention of starting the first beeswax coat this morning. Got into the spare room at 8am with the wax and the cloths laid out. Looked at the board. Something was wrong. Took me a minute to figure out what. The board was sitting flat on the bench but one corner — the upper-right corner if you orient it the way Hugo will when he plays — was slightly off the bench. Not a lot. Maybe 1.5mm of gap. I looked at it from a low angle. The corner is lifted. The board has warped. I pressed down on the corner. It went flat. I lifted my hand. It came back up. The wood remembers. The wood is warping toward something. Sat with this for thirty minutes. Did the diagnostics in my head. The body of the board is laminated cedar. I built it up from three layers of 1cm cedar with the grain alternating, which is supposed to be a stable construction (alternating grain orientation reduces warping). The squares are cedar with walnut inlay strips. The whole thing has been sitting in the spare room for the last two weeks, where the humidity has been low — CDMX is in dry season, ambient humidity has been hovering around 30% for the last week, which is dry by any measure. The likely cause: the spare room has a window that gets afternoon sun and the room is consistently 2-3°C warmer than the rest of the apartment. The board has been sitting there for two weeks while the cedar has been absorbing the warmer/drier conditions, and the inner laminations have shrunk slightly faster than the outer face, which means the surface is now under tension. Differential drying. Classic mistake for a first-time woodworker. I should have left the board in the spare room for two weeks before starting construction, to acclimate, and then built it. Instead I built it from cedar that came straight from the lumberyard at 12% moisture content, and now the cedar has dropped to about 8% in CDMX dry season, and the differential has produced exactly the warp it was always going to produce given how I built it. I called Don Manuel at the lumberyard in Toluca for diagnostic. Described the situation. He confirmed the diagnosis. He said the warp will probably stabilize in another week as the moisture content equalizes through the lamination, but he also said that the warp pattern suggests one of the laminations has a stress that won't fully release. He suggested I look closely at the corner that's lifted and check whether the glue line has any visible separation. I looked. The glue line at the upper-right corner has a thin gap visible from one side. About 2cm long. Hairline width. The glue has not fully held the lamination at that corner. This is bad. This is salvageable but bad. The options as I see them: Option 1: do nothing and see if it stabilizes. Risk: the warp persists or worsens, and the gift to Hugo is a chess board that does not sit flat. This is unacceptable. The board's job is to sit flat. Option 2: clamp the corner flat, inject hide glue into the gap, let it cure for 24 hours, hope that the joint re-bonds at the new geometry. Risk: the moisture differential continues and the board re-warps. Option 3: rebuild the body. Cost: about three weekends of work that I do not have. Hugo's birthday is in four weeks. The math does not allow this option unless something else is also true. Option 4: reverse the differential. Move the board from the warmer spare room to the cooler/more-humid kitchen for several days, let the moisture content rise on the inside surface, and re-clamp the corner during the equalization. Risk: this is essentially option 2 plus some moisture management. I'm going to do a combination of options 2 and 4. Move the board to the kitchen. Let it sit for 48 hours unclamped to absorb a bit more moisture. Then clamp the corner flat, inject hide glue into the gap (Don Manuel said specifically use hide glue, not yellow glue, because hide glue is reversible and won't lock in the wrong position the way a more rigid glue would), let it cure for 24 hours, and reassess. I am not panicking. I am solving the problem. Camila walked in at 9am and asked what was wrong because I had not moved from the bench in an hour. I told her. She said 'Hugo will love it whether or not it's perfectly flat.' Which is the right thing for her to say. But the board is going to be flat. I am going to make it flat. Calling Don Manuel back this afternoon to ask if he has any thoughts on accelerating the moisture equalization safely. Will report back.
