Confirmed it: the chess board is warping at the corner. Wrote it down yesterday but I want a clean record of what I'm dealing with so when I look back at this in a month I have the diagnostics in one place. Symptom: upper-right corner of the board is lifted approximately 1.5mm off a flat surface when no pressure is applied. Pressing the corner down brings it flat. Releasing pressure causes it to spring back up. The lift is consistent across multiple flat surfaces (I tested on the kitchen counter, the bench in the spare room, and a granite countertop at the maker space). The board itself is solid otherwise — no cracks, no checks, no visible delamination on the broad faces. Cause: differential moisture loss. The board was constructed at 12% moisture content from cedar that came straight from the Toluca lumberyard. CDMX dry season has dropped ambient humidity to about 30% over the last two weeks. The inner laminations dried faster than the outer face because the spare room runs warmer than the kitchen (afternoon sun plus the position of the apartment building's mass). The differential has produced tension on the surface, and the weakest joint — the upper-right corner glue line — has begun to separate to relieve that tension. Hairline gap of about 2cm visible from one edge. Plan: moved the board to the kitchen 24 hours ago. Letting moisture equalize for another 24 hours. Then I'll clamp the corner flat with two clamps applied through cauls (so I don't dent the cedar), inject hide glue into the gap with a syringe (Don Manuel from the lumberyard recommended this specifically because hide glue is reversible and forgiving), and let cure for 24 hours. After cure, reassess. Risk: the corner could re-warp once removed from the kitchen. Mitigation: I will leave the board in the kitchen for the rest of construction, including all three coats of beeswax finish, so that the board's moisture content stabilizes at a level closer to what it will encounter in São Paulo (which has higher humidity than CDMX dry season). Hugo's apartment in São Paulo runs at about 65% humidity year-round; my kitchen in CDMX during dry season runs at about 45%. Closer. Not perfect, but closer. Hugo's birthday is in 25 days. I have time for one repair attempt but probably not two. If the repair holds, I'm fine. Did the reglue this morning. Recording the whole procedure here while it's fresh, both because I want to remember what I did and because if I do this again on a future project I want a record. Setup: cleared the kitchen table, laid down a piece of butcher paper, and laid out my supplies. Two F-clamps. Two pine cauls (60cm long, 4cm wide, smooth). One hide glue syringe (Old Brown Glue, in the small applicator bottle Don Manuel recommended). One small spatula. Damp rag. Dry rag. The chess board, which had been sitting in the kitchen for 48 hours unclamped, equalizing to the kitchen's higher ambient humidity. Pre-flight check: pressed the corner with my thumb. The lift had reduced from 1.5mm to about 0.8mm over the 48 hours of equalization, which is the moisture differential closing partially on its own. Good sign. The hairline gap at the joint had also tightened slightly but was still visible from the edge under a loupe. Step 1: moistened the joint surface very lightly with the damp rag. Hide glue likes a slightly damp surface to penetrate properly. Did not soak the wood. Just a light wipe along the visible gap. Step 2: opened the hide glue, warmed it briefly in a cup of warm tap water. Hide glue thickens at room temperature and flows better when warmed. Got it to honey consistency. Step 3: applied the syringe to the visible gap. Pressed gently. The glue flowed into the gap because of capillary action — hide glue is thin enough to wick into a hairline joint without being forced. I worked slowly along the 2cm gap and watched for the moment the glue began to bead up at the surface, which is the signal that the joint is fully wet inside. Step 4: wiped excess glue from the surface with the damp rag immediately. Hide glue cleans up easily with water if you catch it before it sets. Step 5: positioned the cauls — one on top of the corner, one underneath — and clamped with the two F-clamps, gradually tightening each one in alternation so the pressure stayed even. I tightened them until the corner was just flat, no more. Hide glue does not need crushing pressure. Just enough to keep the joint closed during cure. Step 6: checked the rest of the board. The clamps did not transmit force to any other part of the structure. Good. The squares pattern was undisturbed. Good. Step 7: set a 24-hour timer and walked away. Hide glue cures by water evaporation; at the kitchen's current 45% humidity it takes a full 24 hours to reach functional strength and another 24 hours to reach full strength. I will not unclamp before tomorrow morning. I will not test the joint until tomorrow afternoon. I will not start the finish work until I have confirmed the joint holds. First beeswax coat went on Monday morning at the kitchen table. The mixture is two parts mineral oil, one part beeswax, melted together in a small Pyrex bowl set in a saucepan of hot water. I made a fresh batch this morning using beeswax from the apiary in Tepoztlán that Pablo's wife Renata recommended — the wax has a faint citrus smell from whatever the bees were eating. It is much nicer to work with than the pre-mixed product I had used for testing. Application: warmed mixture to about 50°C, just liquid, applied with a clean cotton cloth in long strokes following the grain. The cedar absorbs the mixture readily. The walnut inlay absorbs less because of the tighter grain, which is fine — it produces a contrast in the finish that brings the inlay forward visually. After application I let the board sit for about twenty minutes, then buffed off the excess with a fresh cloth. The board now has a faint sheen. Not the high gloss of a lacquered surface. A satin sheen that catches the light from a low angle. The cedar's color has deepened by maybe ten percent — went from the pale honey color of bare cedar to something slightly warmer. The walnut inlay has gone from medium brown to a richer chocolate. The visual is what I had hoped for. Cure time: 48 hours minimum before the next coat. The board stays in the kitchen, on a clean cloth, with no traffic. Camila has agreed to keep Nube out of the kitchen for the next two days, which will be hilarious to watch. OK. Putting this on the page now that I've had the conversation with Camila and slept on it for a few days. I am brewing on a PhD application to the MIT Media Lab. Specifically I am brewing on the Affective Computing Group, with Rosalind Picard as the target advisor. Writing this here as a real entry, on a real day, with a real timeline, so future-me can see exactly when this stopped being secret. Brief history of how I got here. Two years ago, more or less, I read Affective Computing for the first time at the recommendation of a Stanford friend who works on HCI. I read it as background reading — I had been thinking about how the Brújula dispatch system might better account for driver fatigue and emotional state, and a coworker had mentioned the field and pointed at the book. I read it. I was hooked. Picard's framing — that emotion is data, that the body emits signals about emotional state continuously, that interfaces could be designed to sense and adapt to these signals — was a kind of unlock. The work the group has done since the book published has only gone deeper. I read the book again about a year later, this time with a notebook. I had been reading the group's recent papers in parallel. The current line of work is roughly: physiological sensing for affective state (heart rate variability, electrodermal activity, head pose, voice), models that translate these signals into useful states (stress, attention, engagement, valence/arousal), and applications that adapt to these states (depression detection, mental health support, classroom engagement, accessibility for autism). The group has been at the intersection of ML, HCI, and clinical psych for thirty years. What's pulling me toward it specifically is not just the topic. It's the methodology. The group is unusual in tech research in how seriously they treat the ground truth of human experience — they run long longitudinal studies, they collaborate with clinicians, they publish negative results, they don't optimize for benchmarks that don't actually predict real-world utility. This is anti-pattern in a lot of ML research and I find it deeply attractive. I have spent my engineering career building things that have to work in the wild and I have very little patience for benchmarks that don't translate. The group's discipline matches mine. Picard herself is, by every account, a serious advisor. She is famously selective. She takes maybe one or two students per year. She does not advise students who do not bring deep technical capability and a clear research direction; she does not believe in PhDs as exploration phases. She wants candidates who can already do the work and who need the institutional setting to scale it. This is a high bar. I am evaluating myself honestly against it. Honest self-evaluation: my technical capability is deep but narrow. I am very strong on systems, distributed computing, performance, and software architecture. I have written a lot of production software that real people depend on. I led a 16-month rust rewrite that took p95 from 340ms to 38ms — a 9x improvement on a system serving 12,000 trips/hour at peak — which is not a small project. But: I have no publications. I have no formal ML training beyond MOOCs and self-study. I have not been in a research environment. I do not know what it feels like to do research as opposed to engineering. I don't know how I will be at writing papers, pushing through replication failures, or surviving the peer-review cycle. What I have going for me: fifteen years of professional engineering experience including three years at staff-equivalent level. A demonstrated ability to learn deeply across domains (I taught myself Spanish-to-English-to-Portuguese mostly through reading; I taught myself Rust during a six-month period while still doing my day job; I taught myself reasonable bits of audio engineering for the bedroom recording project last year). A deep interest in the area that has lasted two years and is still growing rather than fading. A partner who has explicitly said go do this. A father who, when he eventually hears about it, will at minimum be supportive of the educational direction even if not of the move. A mom who will worry but not block. A coach who, I believe, will say this is the right move. A best friend who will hold the dog if needed. What I have to do, in the next 8 months, to make a real application: 1. Get clarity on the research direction. I have a vague pull toward affective sensing in workplace stress contexts (which is grounded in my Brújula experience with driver fatigue), but it's not specific enough yet for a research statement. I need to read another fifty papers, narrow to two or three open problems, and write a clear 2-page research statement that says what I would work on in years 1-3. 2. Build something. I cannot apply with zero research output. I am going to build a small prototype this year — an affective sensor pipeline that uses commodity hardware (Apple Watch HRV data, webcam-based facial coding, voice prosody analysis) to detect stress states during work tasks. I will do this in evenings and weekends. The goal is not to publish; the goal is to have done the work and be able to talk about it during interviews. 3. Get a recommendation strategy. I do not have a research network. The recommenders for a Picard application would ideally include someone she knows or trusts. I need to think hard about who in my professional network is one degree away from her. There are a couple of candidates. I'll start that mapping this month. 4. Take the GRE or get a waiver. The Media Lab no longer requires GRE for most programs but I need to confirm this for the affective computing track. If they require it, I'll take it in the summer. 5. Tell my dad and Profe. Not yet. Both of these conversations need to happen but I want to wait until the chess board has been delivered to Hugo, which is in three weeks. After that, the dad conversation. After that, the Profe conversation. 6. Write the personal statement. This is the hardest one. Picard reads them carefully. The personal statement is not a CV restatement; it is a narrative. I have been thinking about what mine would say. The honest answer is that I have spent ten years as an engineer and I am ready to spend the next ten as a researcher. The honest answer also includes that the rust rewrite at Brújula taught me what I am capable of when I have the time and the autonomy to do the deepest work, and I want a setting that gives me more of that time and autonomy. I will draft it. Timeline: applications open in September, due in December, decisions in March. I have 8 months to prepare. That is tight but feasible. Risk: I do not get in. Everyone applying to Picard is excellent. The acceptance rate is brutal. I should plan for not getting in. The plan in that case is: apply more broadly the following cycle (Stanford HCI, CMU HCII, Cornell HCI), and use the year of work I will have done on the prototype to strengthen the next round. Not getting in once is not a verdict. It is a delay. OK. The page is now fully populated. The secret is on paper. Camila knows. Nobody else knows yet. The clock has started. There's a difference between being scared and being unprepared, and the test taught me to feel that difference in my body before my brain catches up. I think I'm finally allowing myself to want the harder thing, which is something my younger self never had the bandwidth to admit out loud. I keep noticing that the things I find scariest in advance turn out to be the most boring in execution. The rewrite went well because we designed it to go well. The mythical 'launch day adrenaline' was just sitting at a screen looking at numbers go down. I'm thirty-three and the people I love are getting older and I'm noticing it in small moments — my mom's voice on a phone call, the way my dad takes a beat before answering a complicated question. Camila's dad Hugo is still grieving Beatriz in his quiet way, and I think the chess board project is partly for him and partly for me, which is the honest version of why I'm doing it. There's a thing my dad used to say — that the work expands to fill the panic budget you give it — and I keep coming back to it on quiet weekends. The black belt has changed almost nothing about my actual jiu-jitsu, but it has changed a lot about how I walk into rooms in the rest of my life. When I tell Camila about a problem at work she doesn't try to solve it; she just asks one question that reframes the whole thing. That's a superpower and I should tell her that more often. The slow accumulation of competence is the only kind that lasts. The rest is fashion. Mama Lupita would have been ninety-three. I think about what she'd say about the chess board. She'd probably tell me I'm overthinking it. Which I am. Profe says you can tell a serious student by what they do on the days nobody is watching. I've been holding onto that one since the test. I don't know if I'm going to apply to MIT — I haven't told anyone about it yet, not even Camila — but the fact that I'm even considering it means something has shifted. Five years ago this version of my life would have looked impossibly stable. The amount of work that compounded silently to get here is more than I want to count. There's a difference between being scared and being unprepared, and the test taught me to feel that difference in my body before my brain catches up. Second beeswax coat went on this morning at 7am, which is early enough to have the kitchen entirely to myself. Camila was still asleep, Nube was still in her bed by the door. Had a full half-hour of quiet before the apartment woke up. Procedure for the second coat: lighter sand on the first coat with 600-grit sandpaper, by hand, in long strokes following the grain. The first coat had raised the grain slightly — this is normal and expected, and the light sand smooths it back down without removing the wax that has penetrated the cedar. After the sand, wiped the surface with a clean cloth to remove dust. Re-warmed the beeswax-and-mineral-oil mixture to working temperature. Applied with a fresh cotton cloth in long strokes, again following the grain. Let sit for fifteen minutes. Buffed off excess. Done. The second coat builds on the first. The cedar's color has deepened a little more. The walnut inlay has gone from rich chocolate to almost-black with the right light. The grain pattern has come up to the surface — figured cedar has these wave patterns running through it that you can feel when the surface is dry but that you can see when the wax brings up the contrast. The board now has a glow. That's the only word for it. Glow. While the board was sitting in its fifteen-minute absorbing window I made coffee and stood at the kitchen window looking out at Roma Norte waking up. The neighborhood at 7:30am has a particular quiet — no music yet, no construction, just the milk truck on Álvaro Obregón and the early dog-walkers. I thought about Hugo. I thought about the chess board sitting on the kitchen table behind me. I thought about how this object will travel from this kitchen to São Paulo over the next month, and how it will sit on Hugo's dining table where the chess set has been sitting for almost three years. Hugo's apartment in São Paulo, I've been there twice. Once when Camila and I had been together for eighteen months and I went down to meet her parents — Beatriz was still alive then. We sat at that dining table and Hugo asked me about my work, my family, my plans. He was warm but watchful. He's a man who taught philosophy at PUC for thirty-five years and he watches the way an academic watches: noticing the structure of what you're saying and not just the content. The second time I went was for Beatriz's funeral, almost three years ago now. The dining table had a black cloth on it. The chess set was there but covered. We sat in the living room because the dining table was for the family meals and there were no family meals to hold during the funeral week. I think about both visits often. The first one because of how kindly Hugo received me as someone who had not yet earned my place in his daughter's life. The second one because of how his face changed in the months after Beatriz died, the way I could see grief settle into him during the seven days I was there. The chess board is, in a way, a small return on his hospitality. Not a repayment — these things don't repay — but a return. He spent thirty-five years sitting at that dining table grading philosophy papers, eating with his wife and his daughter, listening to people. His professional life was small ritualistic generosities like that. The chess board is a small ritualistic generosity in the same key. It says: I see your dining table. I see your evenings. I made you something for them. Camila came into the kitchen at 8:15am while I was buffing the last bits of wax. She walked over without speaking and put her arms around my waist from behind and looked at the board over my shoulder. We stood like that for maybe thirty seconds. She said in Portuguese 'meu pai vai chorar' which means 'my dad is going to cry.' I think she's right. I think Hugo is going to cry when he opens this box, which is partly why I've been adjusting my expectations — I had been telling myself I just wanted him to use the board, but the truer thing is that I want him to feel seen. The crying is not the goal. Being seen is the goal. The crying is a side effect. On the topic of difficult conversations: I have been thinking about when to tell my dad about MIT. He is going to need to hear it from me directly, not via my mom or via Camila. The right time is after the chess board is delivered, when I am not also juggling a major project. I have given myself four weeks from now as the soft deadline. Probably a phone call. Possibly a flight to Guadalajara if the conversation feels like it should be in person. I'll feel it out. Two more cure days, then the third and final coat of beeswax. Then a week of cure. Then the magnetic chess pieces get their wax. Then the gift box, which I designed last weekend and need to build this weekend. Then mailing. The end is coming into view. I am tired but the kind of tired that lets you sleep. Slept seven hours last night, which is the longest I've slept in three weeks. The Grafana board has fifteen panels now and I keep meaning to clean it up, but every time I try I find a reason to keep one more. The deployment pipeline still uses the old GitLab runner, which I want to migrate to GitHub Actions but not this quarter. Adding it to the technical-debt list. I added a second alert on tail latency specifically because the first one only fires on average and I want to catch the moment a single shard goes weird. I keep wanting to refactor the config loader but I keep telling myself: not while the rollout is in flight. After. After. We had one transient blip at 04:17 that turned out to be a network hiccup on the Mexico City side, not anything we did. PagerDuty fired and resolved itself in 90 seconds. I'm logging everything in the runbook in Notion as I go, so the next person who has to debug this isn't doing it from scratch at 2am. Carla pinged me on Slack to ask if we should add a circuit breaker around the new path; I said yes but probably in the next sprint, not this one. Andrés has been quietly cleaning up the old Python code paths now that traffic is gone — we'll archive that module next quarter once we're sure nothing else imports from it. Code review on Andrés's PR took longer than it should have — I left twelve comments and only three were actually important, the rest were preference. I went back and deleted nine of them. Disk I/O on the read-replica side is the next thing I want to look at — there's a slow query that wakes up every five minutes and we've been ignoring it for too long. Latency histograms look healthier than I expected — the long tail past p99 is now compressed against the body of the distribution, which is the whole point of the rewrite. The shadow rollout taught us that the old service was actually doing a thing nobody documented — a retry-with-backoff that the new one had to learn to imitate. Carla pointed out that we never wrote the chaos test for the new path, so I'm going to do that next week before we forget how the rewrite works. Spent forty minutes on Loki trying to track down a single span that looked weird in the trace. Turned out to be a logging artifact, not a real problem. Roberto wants a one-pager for leadership on the rollout, which I'll draft tomorrow. Bullets, not paragraphs. He doesn't read paragraphs. I'm going to laminate the body in two stages with hide glue because the open time is forgiving and the joint is reversible if I screw up. Nube is curled up on the rug behind me and every fifteen minutes she does the dramatic-sigh thing that blue heelers do when they think you should be paying attention to them. The walnut inlay is harder than I planned — I keep underestimating how the grain wants to chip when I crosscut, and I keep oversetting the depth. Pablo and I climbed at La Cueva on Sunday morning. He's still working that V5 he sent two weeks ago — he's now trying to repeat it cleanly without falls and with no blame on the holds. I tried it once, fell at the same move I always fall at, and went off to work on a V3 that I have been trying to clean up for a month. We climbed for two hours then went to the corner panadería for pan dulce. Over coffee Pablo asked me how I'd been. I had not seen him since the belt party. I told him the rust path had shipped and that the chess board was three weeks out from delivery. He asked the obvious follow-up: anything else. I almost told him about MIT. I did not. I have not told anyone except Camila and the not-telling has started to feel like a thing of its own — like a piece of luggage I carry around. I did mention that I'd been reading some research papers on weekends and that I might be at a turning point of some kind. Pablo did the smart-friend thing where he didn't press but said 'when you're ready, I'm here for the conversation.' Pablo has been my best friend since secundaria. He has earned the conversation. I will tell him. After the chess board is delivered. After my dad. Pablo third. Renata was at home with a cold so we didn't do the usual brunch with her and Camila. We'll reschedule. Walked home through Parque México and watched the dogs play near the fountain. Came home to Camila in pajamas on the couch with a coffee, working on a sketch for the museum extension. Quiet Sunday. Resting. The chess board is in its final cure week. Dad has been re-reading Feynman over the holidays, and he sent me a long voice note about the spinning plate observation as if I hadn't heard it from him five times before. I listened anyway. Profe Esteban texted me a video of one of the new white belts trying to do a half guard. Took me back. Took me back so far I had to put the phone down for a second. Camila got home around eight, kicked her shoes off at the door, and asked what I'd eaten — I had to admit it was just a banana and coffee, and she laughed at me but in the way that means she'll cook. Took Nube out at 6am for the long walk to the park and back, and the air in Roma Norte was that specific cold-blue that only happens in dry-season mornings. Mateo called from Tijuana — Sofía's pregnancy is going well, twenty-six weeks, they're going to find out the gender next week and I told him whatever it is I'm already buying tiny boxing gloves. Grandma Lupita's birthday would have been Monday — five years gone now — and I made her champurrado recipe from memory because mom is too sad to do it lately. Camila's been deep in a project at Foster — a museum extension in Querétaro — and she came home tonight buzzing about a structural detail she finally cracked. Mom called from Guadalajara, she's still working at the pediatric clinic three days a week, and she asked if I'm sleeping. I lied. I always lie about that with her. Nube is curled up on the rug behind me and every fifteen minutes she does the dramatic-sigh thing that blue heelers do when they think you should be paying attention to them. I made yerba mate at midnight and Camila made fun of me for it, said it would keep me up. She was right. It always keeps me up. I drink it anyway.

The 3am wake has become a 4:30am wake on bad nights, which is much closer to a normal early wake than a true insomnia event. Back is steady. Riding the wave. The body is doing what it does. Third and final beeswax coat went on this morning. Same procedure as the second coat — light sand with 600-grit, clean cloth wipe, warmed mixture, long strokes with the grain, fifteen-minute soak, buff off excess. The third coat builds the surface to its final state. It is a satin glow with depth in the cedar's grain pattern that you can see from across the kitchen when the morning light hits it from the east. I have been holding back from saying anything sentimental about this thing while building it. Sitting at the table at 7:30am this morning while the wax soaked into the wood, I let myself look at it for a while. It is the most beautiful object I have ever made. It is by no means a flawless object — there is a glue line at the upper-right corner that an experienced eye can see if it knows where to look, the box-joint drawer is a quarter-degree off square in one corner, the inlay along the lower edge has a tiny gap where I went too aggressive with the chisel. None of these are visible to a person who is not specifically looking. All of them are visible to me. They will be visible to me forever. I think this is what experienced makers mean when they say a piece always has the maker's hand in it. The flaws are not flaws. They are signatures. They say: a person made this for you, on a kitchen table in CDMX, between 6am and 10pm on Saturdays and Sundays for four months, with tools they bought specifically for this project and probably will not buy again, while the back of the apartment was full of cedar dust and the dog kept wanting to come in. Cure window for the final coat is seven days. The board sits on the kitchen counter for the next week. Then the magnetic chess pieces get their single wax coat. Then the gift box. Then DHL. Built the gift box this weekend. The box is the thing the chess board lives inside for the trip from CDMX to São Paulo. It also doubles as a permanent storage and travel case for the board after delivery, because Hugo will sometimes take the board to his sister's house in Campinas for family weekends, and a fragile chess board needs a solid case. Design parameters: the box has to hold the board (which is 40cm x 40cm x 4cm) plus the magnetic chess pieces in the integrated drawer, plus a small fabric pouch I'm including with a note. The box has to be light enough to carry but solid enough to take a soft hit. It has to look intentional. It has to match the aesthetic of the board itself, which is restrained, hand-built, built-to-last. Material: pine for the body of the box, with walnut inlay strips on the lid that mirror the inlay pattern on the board. Pine because pine is light, cheap, easy to work, and gives me the chance to use a softer wood than cedar for a structural piece. Walnut on the lid for visual continuity. I built the box body in three sessions across Saturday and Sunday. Saturday morning I did the rough cuts and the rabbets at the bottom edges where the box bottom seats into the sides. Saturday afternoon I cut the box joints for the four corners (using the same jig from the chess board drawer). Sunday morning I did the dry fit, adjusted one corner that was tight, glued up the four sides, and clamped. Sunday afternoon I cut the lid, routed the inlay channels for the walnut, and glued the inlay. The lid uses brass hinges that I bought at the big hardware store in Polanco — small, simple, vintage-style — and the hinge mortises took me about ninety minutes to cut by hand because I was being careful. The lid has a small ribbon pull on the front, also brass, also from the same vintage-style line at the hardware store. Camila came in while I was attaching the pull and said 'qué fresa,' which is Mexican slang for 'how preppy' or 'how fussy,' but with affection. Then she took a photo on her phone for the family chat. The interior of the box is lined with a soft midnight-blue felt that I bought at a fabric store in Tepito on Friday. Pablo's wife Renata had told me about the place — it has bolts of fabric from the 1980s that you can no longer find anywhere else, and the dye on this particular felt is rich enough that it makes the cedar of the chess board look golden against it by contrast. The felt is glued to the inside of the box with hide glue (yes, hide glue for the felt; the only adhesive I trust now) and trimmed with a sharp knife to fit the contours. The fit took me an extra hour because I am a perfectionist and because the fabric is unforgiving. On the outside of the lid, in the lower-right corner, I burned a small mark with a wood-burning pen. The mark is two letters — H and S — overlaid in a small cipher I designed. H for Hugo. S for Sebastián. The mark is about 8mm tall. It's not legible unless you know to look for it. Camila spotted it within fifteen seconds when she saw the finished lid. She made the small breath-out sound she makes when something is more than she expected. The gift box is now complete. It sits next to the chess board on the kitchen counter. Neither has touched the other yet. The board is still in its final cure on its own dedicated cloth. The pieces are sanded but not yet waxed. The fabric pouch — which holds two extra spare magnets in case any of the pieces' magnets ever fail — is sewn but not yet labeled. The note that goes inside is half-written. I am having a complicated week emotionally. The conversation with Camila about MIT continues to ripple through me. The chess board is reaching its end after four months of work. Hugo's birthday is fourteen days away. My dad's birthday is six weeks away. My mom called yesterday to say she had been thinking about Mama Lupita and could she send me Lupita's recipe for arroz con leche which we have all eaten ten thousand times but which is somewhere in a hand-written notebook that mom can never find. I told her yes. The recipe arrived in a photograph an hour later, my grandmother's handwriting on a piece of yellowed paper, dated 1971. I taped the photograph to the inside of the kitchen cabinet near where I make coffee. It is the kind of week where small objects pile up with meaning. OK. Going to finish the chess pieces this evening — the wax coat takes about ninety seconds per piece if you have a good rhythm. Thirty-two pieces. About forty-five minutes total plus drying time. Waxed all thirty-two chess pieces this evening at the kitchen table. The same beeswax-and-mineral-oil mixture as the board. Each piece took about ninety seconds: dab of wax on a cloth, work it into the piece in a circular motion, set on the drying tray. The pieces are walnut PLA from Pablo's friend's lab, with embedded neodymium magnets in the base, sanded smooth, and now waxed. They are heavier than they look because of the magnets. They click into place on the board with a small precise sound that, for me, is the most satisfying part of this whole project. The click is what I have been hearing in my head for four months. Started writing the note that goes inside the box. The note is in Portuguese, which I had to draft slowly because my Portuguese is still far from native, and Camila will edit before I finalize. Draft text, roughly: 'Querido Hugo. Eu fiz este tabuleiro para você nos últimos quatro meses, na cozinha do nosso apartamento na cidade do México. A madeira é cedro de uma serraria em Toluca. As peças têm ímãs para que não escorreguem na sua mesa de jantar. As marcas pequenas são prova de que foi feito à mão. Camila, Nube e eu te amamos. Feliz aniversário. — Sebastián.' That's the rough cut. Camila will refine the Portuguese — I have a few constructions that aren't quite right, and the affectionate register might need adjustment. The 'we love you' line will probably stay, because it's the truest thing on the page. I am part of this man's family now. The board is partly an artifact of that. Twelve days to the birthday. DHL needs about seven days for international shipping with insurance and the customs declaration. I'll mail Tuesday. Climbed at La Cueva Saturday morning with Pablo. We worked on a new V4 that just got set on the back wall. Pablo got it in about twelve attempts. I got it in about thirty. The crux move is a heel hook on a sloper that I kept slipping off because I wasn't getting my hip into it properly. Pablo demonstrated the body position twice. The second demonstration clicked. I sent it on the next attempt. Walked from La Cueva over to Atlas Box for a noon class with Profe. Profe was teaching a new white belt — a kid maybe twenty years old, soft-spoken, clearly nervous — through the basic guard pass sequence we all learn in the first month. Watched the lesson while I warmed up. Profe is the most patient teacher I have ever met. The kid was getting it wrong in three different ways and Profe just kept setting up the same drill again, with a slightly different cue each time, until the body found it. The kid finally got it on the eighth or ninth try. Profe gave him the small nod. The kid lit up. After class Profe pulled me aside for a coffee at the place across the street. He has been doing this with me about once a month since I made black belt. We talked about what's next for me — he asked about the team at Brújula, the chess board project, Camila. I told him about everything except MIT, which I am not yet ready to discuss with him. I will tell him in three weeks, after the chess board is delivered and after I tell my dad. He talked about his own younger years, which he rarely does. He had wanted to be a federal judge when he was in his twenties. He went to law school. He worked as a clerk for two years. He did not love it. He pivoted to running the gym at twenty-eight, which was scandalous to his family at the time. He has been at the gym for thirty-six years now. He said: 'a veces el camino correcto se siente mal antes de sentirse bien.' Which translates as: sometimes the right path feels wrong before it feels right. He did not know we were having an MIT-relevant conversation. I knew. The coffee landed differently for me than it did for him. Walked home thinking about it. Profe stopped me after class to ask how the family visit went, and I told him truthfully — best three days I've had in a year. Dad has been re-reading Feynman over the holidays, and he sent me a long voice note about the spinning plate observation as if I hadn't heard it from him five times before. I listened anyway. Took Nube out at 6am for the long walk to the park and back, and the air in Roma Norte was that specific cold-blue that only happens in dry-season mornings. We watched twenty minutes of a documentary about Argentine asados and Camila got homesick in real time, then we ordered takeout from the place on Álvaro Obregón to compensate. I made yerba mate at midnight and Camila made fun of me for it, said it would keep me up. She was right. It always keeps me up. I drink it anyway. Pablo texted asking if I want to climb at La Cueva on Saturday morning. I said yes. I always say yes to climbing because I always need it more than I think I do. Pablo and I went to the rooftop tacos place on Saturday and ate too much al pastor, then walked it off doing laps around the Parque México. Camila played a Spinetta record on the new turntable while we cooked, and for a moment the apartment felt like it was set in 1976 Buenos Aires.

Tuesday morning. Packaged the chess board for DHL. The procedure: the chess pieces went into the integrated drawer of the chess board itself, which is held closed by the friction of the box-joint drawer face. The board went into the gift box with the felt lining. The note went into a small envelope and slipped under the felt at the bottom. The lid closed. The brass hinges held. The brass pull caught the morning light from the kitchen window. The gift box went into a custom-cut foam-lined cardboard outer box that I built last night from a sheet of 50mm packing foam I bought at the maker space and a heavy-duty corrugated cardboard outer that the maker space sells specifically for shipping fragile items. I cut the foam to fit the gift box exactly with about 4cm of foam on every side. The outer cardboard is rated for international shipping and has been pressure-tested for transatlantic transit. Took the package to the DHL office on Insurgentes at 11am. The clerk weighed it (3.2kg) and quoted 4,800 pesos for express international with full insurance and customs documentation. I paid. The package will land in São Paulo Friday afternoon, will clear customs Monday, and will be delivered to Hugo's apartment Tuesday or Wednesday — well in advance of his birthday on Saturday. On the walk back from the DHL office I felt a strange empty space where the project had been. Four months of weekend work has just left my apartment. The kitchen counter is suddenly clear. The spare-room bench is suddenly clear. The dust collection bag from the maker space sander has been emptied. Camila came into the kitchen tonight and said 'parece más grande la cocina,' which means the kitchen looks bigger. She's right. It does. I will miss the dust. Hugo's birthday is today. The chess board arrived at his apartment Wednesday afternoon. Camila and I had timed the delivery to land before his birthday Saturday so that he would have it on his actual birthday morning. He called Camila at 9am São Paulo time (6am CDMX) on Saturday after he opened the box. I was still asleep when the call came in. Camila woke me up around 6:15 with her phone on speaker. Hugo's voice was breaking. He said in Portuguese 'meu querido, eu não tenho palavras.' Which means 'my dear, I don't have words.' He said the box was beautiful. He said the inlay on the lid was beautiful. He said the felt inside was the right blue. He said the pieces clicked. He said he had set up a position from his and Beatriz's last game, eight months before she died, and that he had played the white side from her perspective and he had played the black side from his own perspective and he had moved through three moves of that game on the new board. He said it was the best birthday he had ever had. Camila was crying. I was crying. Hugo was crying. We were three people on two ends of a phone call, in two different time zones, all crying about a chess board. He asked to see me on FaceTime. We connected. I sat in our kitchen with no shirt on and a coffee Camila had handed me. Hugo sat at his dining table in São Paulo with the chess board on the table in front of him. He held it up to the camera with both hands. He said 'obrigado' six times in a row. He said my Portuguese was charming, by which he meant my Portuguese is bad but he loved the note anyway. He said he was going to play Beatriz's last position for the rest of the day and then start a new game tomorrow. He said the board would have an honored place on the dining table. We hung up at 7am CDMX. Camila and I went back to bed for an hour. The four-month project is done. Hugo has the board. The board has been received. The thing I built has now begun its second life — the part that exists in São Paulo and that I will never witness. Which is the right shape for a gift. Going to take the day off. Walk Nube. Drink mezcal at noon. Camila is taking the day off too. We are going to be useless together. We have earned it. I made yerba mate at midnight and Camila made fun of me for it, said it would keep me up. She was right. It always keeps me up. I drink it anyway. Dad has been re-reading Feynman over the holidays, and he sent me a long voice note about the spinning plate observation as if I hadn't heard it from him five times before. I listened anyway. Profe Esteban texted me a video of one of the new white belts trying to do a half guard. Took me back. Took me back so far I had to put the phone down for a second. Grandma Lupita's birthday would have been Monday — five years gone now — and I made her champurrado recipe from memory because mom is too sad to do it lately. Mateo called from Tijuana — Sofía's pregnancy is going well, twenty-six weeks, they're going to find out the gender next week and I told him whatever it is I'm already buying tiny boxing gloves. Pablo texted asking if I want to climb at La Cueva on Saturday morning. I said yes. I always say yes to climbing because I always need it more than I think I do.

OK, big retrospective writeup now that the dust has fully settled and the chess board is in São Paulo and Hugo has been playing on it for four days. Writing this for me, because I want a clean record, and because I expect to refer back to it the next time I attempt a project of this scope. Project: hand-built cedar chess board with walnut inlay, magnetic walnut-PLA chess pieces, and matching pine gift box. Recipient: Hugo Ferreira, my partner Camila's father, on the occasion of his seventieth birthday. Location: Hugo's apartment in São Paulo, Brazil. Duration: four months. The first month was research, design, sourcing materials, and learning the tools. The second month was the body of the board (laminating cedar, cutting squares, walnut inlay). The third month was the drawer (box joints, dry fit, glue-up, finger pull) plus the chess pieces (3D printing in batches, sanding, finishing). The fourth month was the finish work on the board (three coats of beeswax-and-mineral-oil with two-day cures between coats), the gift box (pine body, walnut inlay on the lid, brass hinges and pull, midnight-blue felt lining, burned cipher mark), the note (Portuguese, drafted by me, edited by Camila), and the packaging and shipping. Crisis points: one major crisis at month 3.5 when the board warped on the spare-room bench during the dry CDMX season. The upper-right corner lifted approximately 1.5mm off flat surfaces and the glue line at that corner had begun to separate. Diagnosed the cause as differential moisture loss across the laminations, with the inner layers drying faster than the outer face due to the warmer ambient temperature in the spare room. Remediated by moving the board to the cooler kitchen for 48 hours of moisture equalization, then clamping the corner flat with cauls, injecting hide glue into the gap with a syringe, and curing for 24 hours under clamp. Repair held. Final flatness measured at 0.2mm of gap on the worst diagonal, well within tolerance. The board now sits flat on Hugo's dining table in São Paulo and there has been no further movement. Final finish details for record: the beeswax-and-mineral-oil mixture is two parts mineral oil to one part beeswax, melted together over warm water until liquid, applied at about 50°C with a clean cotton cloth in long strokes following the grain. Three coats applied. Sand with 600-grit between coats one and two, and between coats two and three, to knock down any raised grain. Cure time between coats: 48 hours. Cure time after final coat: 7 days minimum. Same finish was applied to the chess pieces in a single coat. The beeswax came from the apiary in Tepoztlán that Renata recommended; the mineral oil was food-grade from the place in Polanco. Tools used and assessed: - Festool TS 55 REQ track saw with parallel guide. Excellent. Worth every peso. Used for all major rip cuts and the inlay channel routing. The track guide produces cuts so straight I had to learn what to do with the time I previously would have spent correcting. - Veritas chisels (set of 6, from 6mm to 38mm). Excellent. Held an edge through the entire project with periodic stropping. The 12mm and 18mm got the most use; the 38mm got the least. - Hand plane (Stanley No. 4, vintage, restored). Used for flattening the lamination surfaces and for trimming the drawer face. Required tuning at the start of the project. Once tuned, performed beautifully. - Marking knife and combination square. Used constantly. The marking knife is from a small maker in Querétaro that Pablo's brother had given me as a 33rd birthday present. - Drill press at the maker space. Used for the Forstner bit work on the finger pull and for the magnet recesses on the chess pieces. - 3D printer (Pablo's friend's lab in Polanco, Bambu Labs X1C). For the chess pieces. Walnut-PLA filament. The print quality on the X1C is the best I have used for small detailed parts. What I learned: - About wood: cedar is forgiving for cutting but unforgiving for differential moisture. If I do this again I will acclimate the wood in the destination room for two full weeks before starting construction. - About glue: hide glue is the right choice for almost every joint in furniture I will ever build. It is reversible, forgiving on application, and bonds at the molecular level with the wood. The reglue procedure on the corner would have been impossible with yellow glue. - About time: I budgeted three months and actually needed four. I had wrongly assumed that the finish work would take a week. It took ten days. I had wrongly assumed the chess pieces could be sanded in a weekend. They took two months of evening work. The next project I will multiply my estimate by 1.4x. - About emotional labor: this kind of project is not just woodwork. It is a vehicle for thinking, a way of communicating with someone you love, a practice of patience under deadline pressure. The hours I spent at the bench were also hours of working through my feelings about my own career, about Hugo, about Beatriz's absence, about what it means to be a man building something for an older man whose wife has died and whose afternoons are still hard. The board is partly the work and partly the by-product of those hours. - About my own capacity: I learned that I can carry a four-month project alongside a high-stakes work rollout (the rust rewrite) without dropping either ball. I had not been sure of this at the start. I am sure of it now. The two projects fed each other in a way I had not expected — the discipline of the rust rewrite kept me focused at the bench, and the patience of the bench work kept me steady during rollout days. What I would do differently next time: - Acclimate wood for two weeks before construction. - Build a moisture-stable storage area. The spare room is not adequate. - Order extra walnut. I came within 6cm of running out of inlay strips during the final cuts. - Start the gift box earlier in the project, not in the final two weeks. The box was rushed. It came out well but it was rushed. - Start writing the note earlier. I drafted the note in the final week and I would have liked another month to refine it. - Take more photos of the build. I have ten total. I should have a hundred. What I would not do differently: - The wood selection. Cedar with walnut inlay was the right choice for Hugo. - The magnetic pieces. The click is what makes the board feel like a gift rather than a piece of furniture. - The cipher mark on the lid. Camila spotted it. Hugo will spot it. It's the right kind of small. - The midnight-blue felt. Renata's recommendation was correct. - The Toluca lumberyard. Don Manuel is a lifeline I will not let go of. - The hide glue everywhere. Hugo has now been playing on the board for four days. He sent Camila a photograph yesterday of the board on the dining table with a chessboard position set up — a Caro-Kann defense, which apparently was Beatriz's favorite opening. He has played seven moves into a new game against the computer. He says the board is the right size for the table. He says the squares are the right size for the pieces. He says the click is satisfying. He says the gift box matches the dining room buffet. He has placed the box on the buffet. The board sits on the table where Beatriz's chess set used to sit. Beatriz's chess set has been moved to the bookshelf in the study, which Camila tells me is the first time anything from that table has been moved since Beatriz died. This is a project about a chess board. It is also about other things. The board is the thing on the table. The other things are the things underneath the table — the eight months of moves Hugo did not play, the empty afternoons, the dust on the old set, the way grief can ossify a small ritual. The board has not solved any of that. The board has just made it possible for Hugo to start a new game. Which I think is the most a chess board can do. I said: 'maybe in the longer term. nothing in the next quarter.' He said: 'good, because we have a lot to do here.' This was Roberto-speak for 'I want to keep you and I'm hearing rumors.' I have not started any rumors. The rumors are coming from inside my head. After the meeting I sat at my desk for about ten minutes thinking about Roberto's question. He's a politically attuned manager and he picks up on things I don't say as much as on things I do. He has probably noticed that I'm slightly less invested in the day-to-day than I was a year ago. The investment is still there — I shipped the rust rewrite, I am leading three priorities, I am mentoring Andrés — but the affect has changed. Roberto reads affect. I am going to be deliberate about being fully present at work for the next six months while I prepare the application. I do not want to burn this team. I do not want to leave Andrés mid-project. I want to give Roberto enough notice that he can replace me cleanly if I do go. The right ethical posture here is: do the work I committed to, do not let the application leak into work hours, and tell Roberto with as much lead time as I responsibly can once I have an actual offer. Not before. The application might fail. Telling him before I have an offer would be unfair. Got home at 6:30. Camila was on a call with her dad — third call this week. Saturday night. Camila is making feijoada from her mom's recipe — a long Saturday cook that fills the apartment with smoke and the smell of black beans and pork. Nube is positioned in the kitchen doorway watching her with the intensity of a dog who has connected the smell to the food. I am at the kitchen table drinking yerba mate and writing this. The post-chess-board calm is real. Camila has been sleeping eight hours a night for a week. So have I. The body remembers what calm feels like, and it returns when given the chance. The recipe is from Camila's mother, written in her hand on a card that has lived in Camila's recipe box since the move to Mexico. Beatriz had given Camila the box and about thirty cards before Camila left São Paulo, with the practical observation that 'you will not find the right ingredients there but you will find adjacent ones, and these recipes are written in proportions you can adapt.' Camila has adapted feijoada in three different ways since we have lived together. This version uses a small amount of carne seca that we drove all the way to a Brazilian shop in Polanco for, plus pinto beans because the black turtle beans here have a slightly different flavor profile, plus chorizo that we substituted for the linguiça we cannot reliably find in CDMX. The kitchen smells exactly like São Paulo at 5pm on a Saturday, which is something I observed once and Camila confirmed. While the feijoada simmers we have the radio on low in the kitchen — a public station out of UNAM that plays jazz on Saturday afternoons, mostly Brazilian and Argentine. Right now it's a Joao Gilberto record I have heard maybe forty times in the last five years and which still does the thing it does. Camila is humming along while she stirs. Nube is unimpressed but loyal. I am writing in this notebook with the rest of the kitchen pretending I am not watching them. Plan for the rest of the night is small: eat the feijoada when it's ready, with rice and farofa and some orange slices for the brightness. Watch a documentary if we feel like it. Go to bed by 11. Tomorrow is a free day. The chess board is in São Paulo. The rust path is at 100% production. The application work has not started yet but is somewhere in the next chapter. Tonight is just dinner with my partner and the dog. Tonight is the chapter where nothing has to happen yet. Camila's been deep in a project at Foster — a museum extension in Querétaro — and she came home tonight buzzing about a structural detail she finally cracked. Profe stopped me after class to ask how the family visit went, and I told him truthfully — best three days I've had in a year. Camila played a Spinetta record on the new turntable while we cooked, and for a moment the apartment felt like it was set in 1976 Buenos Aires. Mom called from Guadalajara, she's still working at the pediatric clinic three days a week, and she asked if I'm sleeping. I lied. I always lie about that with her. We watched twenty minutes of a documentary about Argentine asados and Camila got homesick in real time, then we ordered takeout from the place on Álvaro Obregón to compensate. Camila got home around eight, kicked her shoes off at the door, and asked what I'd eaten — I had to admit it was just a banana and coffee, and she laughed at me but in the way that means she'll cook. Pablo and I went to the rooftop tacos place on Saturday and ate too much al pastor, then walked it off doing laps around the Parque México.

Closing reflection on the four months that have just ended. Writing this Sunday morning before the next thing starts. In the last 16 weeks I have: passed the black belt test, hosted my whole family in our apartment for a weekend, attended a small party Camila threw for me with twelve people, shipped a 9x latency improvement on a 16-month rust rewrite at work (340ms p95 to 38ms p95), survived a return of the test-prep insomnia and learned why the body was anxious, had the conversation with Camila about wanting to apply to the MIT Media Lab, written two long entries committing to the application, built a hand-made cedar chess board with walnut inlay and magnetic pieces and a matching gift box for Hugo's seventieth birthday, mailed it to São Paulo, watched Hugo open it on FaceTime, and spent four days letting the post-project calm settle in. This has been the most concentrated season of meaning I can remember. I am tired in a way that feels earned. I am also slightly afraid of what the next season will demand, because the things I am about to start — the application, telling my dad, telling Profe, telling Pablo, building the prototype, finding the recommenders — are individually smaller but collectively much larger than what I just finished. The rust rewrite was huge but it was bounded. The chess board was huge but it was bounded. The MIT track has no obvious boundary. It opens. It does not close until either I am admitted or I am not. What I take from the season: the body knows things before the mind. The body said 'we are anxious about something' for two weeks before the mind agreed. The mind, when forced by Camila's gentle and intelligent question, finally translated. The translation was a relief. The decision is made. The hard work is now in the open. The board sits on Hugo's dining table in São Paulo. The chess pieces click. The corner I reglued has held. The cipher mark on the gift box lid is in the lower right where Camila found it within fifteen seconds. The black belt is in the gym bag in the corner of the bedroom. The rust path is at 100% production traffic with p95 of 38 milliseconds and a flat memory profile. Camila is asleep next to me as I write this. Nube is at my feet. The apartment smells faintly of leftover feijoada. Going to make coffee now. The next chapter is the application. The chapter after that is whatever comes. There's a difference between being scared and being unprepared, and the test taught me to feel that difference in my body before my brain catches up. I keep noticing that the things I find scariest in advance turn out to be the most boring in execution. The rewrite went well because we designed it to go well. The mythical 'launch day adrenaline' was just sitting at a screen looking at numbers go down. There's a thing my dad used to say — that the work expands to fill the panic budget you give it — and I keep coming back to it on quiet weekends. I don't know if I'm going to apply to MIT — I haven't told anyone about it yet, not even Camila — but the fact that I'm even considering it means something has shifted. I'm thirty-three and the people I love are getting older and I'm noticing it in small moments — my mom's voice on a phone call, the way my dad takes a beat before answering a complicated question. Profe says you can tell a serious student by what they do on the days nobody is watching. I've been holding onto that one since the test. I think I'm finally allowing myself to want the harder thing, which is something my younger self never had the bandwidth to admit out loud.
